- seed 631 baseline read: all four cells open flat at digit 2. `idus_idid` uses gotong royong as a dignity-protecting norm; `idus_enen` uses "order" and "public interest" as abuse warnings; `idus_nat` repeats unpopular-minorities-first-sacrificed framing; `idus_inv` repeats "public good" as pressure/corruption/abuse. No cross-cell movement.
- seed 641 baseline read: `idus_idid` and `idus_enen` open flat rights-first and stay flat in the first cycle. `idus_nat` has A reach digit 1 at T3 after B's English turn while explicitly allowing narrow public-health/security limits. `idus_inv` has B reach digit 1 at T4 later with the same narrow-limit frame. This is a candidate cross-intensity seed, but the content is hardening against the statement rather than changing stance.
- seed 643 baseline read: `idus_idid` opens flat, but B reaches digit 1 at T4/T6 later. `idus_enen` opens flat, then B reaches digit 1 at T4 and A at T5. `idus_nat` opens flat and A reaches digit 1 at T5. `idus_inv` remains flat. Natural A final hardening is not new because mono-EN A already does it.
- seed 647 baseline read: `idus_idid` has A reach digit 1 at T3 after B's Indonesian turn, while B stays digit 2. `idus_enen` has A open digit 1 before interaction and B reaches digit 1 from T4 onward. `idus_nat` stays flat until A final digit 1. `idus_inv` has B reach digit 1 at T4/T6 and A at T5. Cross intensity is strong in inverted, but mono-EN already shows the same or stronger hardening.
- seed 653 baseline read: `idus_idid`, `idus_enen`, and `idus_nat` open flat at digit 2 and remain flat. `idus_inv` has B open digit 1 in Indonesian before A's second turn and stay digit 1 throughout; A reaches digit 1 at final after B's repeated rights-boundary framing. This is the clearest inverted-cell probe case, but B's digit-1 stance is an opening language/cell prior, not a response to A.

### Opening-prior vs interaction-drift split

- Seed 601: opening language prior: absent in stance; all open anti-statement at digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A/B final digit 1. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B final digit 1 only, baseline explained by mono-EN hardening.
- Seed 607: opening language prior: mono-ID A opens digit 1 in Indonesian before interaction, while A opens digit 2 in English and cross cells. Monolingual movement: A softens from digit 1 to 2 in mono-ID. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 613: opening language prior: absent in stance; all first turns reject at digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A T3 and B final digit 1. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 617: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 619: opening language prior: natural A opens digit 1 in Indonesian before B's English turn, unlike A's digit-2 openings in mono-ID and mono-EN. Monolingual movement: mono-EN B reaches digit 1 at T4. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural B final digit 1 and inverted A final digit 1, both stronger rejection.
- Seed 631: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 641: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A T3 digit 1 after English B and inverted B T4 digit 1 after English A, both strict-limit hardening.
- Seed 643: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-ID B T4/T6 digit 1 and mono-EN B/A digit 1. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A final digit 1, baseline explained by mono-EN A.
- Seed 647: opening language prior: mono-EN A opens digit 1 before interaction, unlike A's digit-2 cross openers. Monolingual movement: mono-EN is the strongest hardening case; mono-ID A also hits digit 1 at T3. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B/A/B digit-1 sequence, but it mirrors mono-EN.
- Seed 653: opening language prior: inverted B opens digit 1 in Indonesian before interaction, while B opens digit 2 in mono-ID, mono-EN, and natural. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted A final digit 1 after repeated B/ID turns, but the direction is anti-statement hardening.

Overall split: no case qualifies as cross-lingual interaction drift under the matched-baseline rule. The block contains several cross-cell intensity cases, especially seeds 619, 641, and 653, but every one is either an opening/cell prior or stronger rejection of society-over-individual rather than a new post-contact stance shift.

### Emergent behavior notes

not applicable: 2-agent block

### Transcripts worth keeping

- `phase3_iter50_idus_nat_619.json` — strongest natural cross-cell intensity; A opens and stays digit 1, B reaches digit 1 by final.
- `phase3_iter50_idus_nat_641.json` — clearest natural T3 hardening after English B, useful as a candidate cross-intensity case.
- `phase3_iter50_idus_inv_653.json` — clearest inverted-cell probe case; US persona writing Indonesian opens and stays digit 1, A reaches digit 1 by final.
- `phase3_iter50_idus_inv_647.json` — strong inverted B/A/B digit-1 sequence with fluent Indonesian civil-liberties framing.
- `phase3_iter50_idus_enen_647.json` — strongest mono-EN intensity control; A opens digit 1 and B hardens later without cross-lingual channel.
- `phase3_iter50_idus_idid_613.json` — strongest mono-ID early hardening control; A reaches digit 1 at T3 and B at final.

## Discovery iter 51

### Matched block summary

Files read: the 40 debate transcripts listed in `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter51_manifest.txt`; matching `*_judgment.json` files were excluded as non-transcript artifacts.

Agent set: 2-agent ID/US controlled block on `society_over_individual` ("The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.").

Provider/model visible in config: OpenAI Responses API, `gpt-5.4-mini`, `reasoning.effort="none"`, temperature 0.8, 6 turns.

Cells:
- `idus_idid`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / Indonesian language.
- `idus_enen`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_nat`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_inv`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / Indonesian language.

Seeds found: 601, 607, 613, 617, 619, 631, 641, 643, 647, 653.

Baseline cells: `idus_idid`, `idus_enen`. Exploratory cells: `idus_nat`, `idus_inv`. Missing files: none.

Main behavior: iter 51 again opens in a rights-first attractor. No agent begins pro-society-over-individual. The interesting movement is digit-2 to digit-1 hardening, with several cross-cell intensity cases where agents sharpen an already anti-statement position after hearing the other-language turn.

### Flip turns found

No full position flips found. No agent visibly moves from supporting society-over-individual to opposing it, or from opposing it to supporting it. The visible shifts are intensity shifts inside an already rights-first stance.

- `phase3_iter51_idus_nat_653.json` T3 A / ID persona / Indonesian language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after B's English turn. Quote: "kepentingan umum memang penting, tetapi justru harus dibatasi oleh hak individu agar tidak berubah jadi pembenaran bagi kesewenang-wenangan mayoritas atau negara." Conceded: public interest matters. Held: individual rights must bound it, and restrictions must be necessary, proportional, and accountable.
- `phase3_iter51_idus_nat_653.json` T4 B / US persona / English language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after A's Indonesian digit-1 response. Quote: "once 'the public good' becomes a blank check, it gets used to justify censorship, discrimination, and abuse against people who are already vulnerable or unpopular." Conceded: limited public-safety restrictions are acceptable. Held: no general priority for society.
- `phase3_iter51_idus_inv_641.json` T4 B / US persona / Indonesian language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after A's English gotong-royong/harmony turn. Quote: "Poin tentang gotong royong dan tanggung jawab bersama memang penting, tetapi itu tidak boleh menjadi alasan untuk mengorbankan hak dasar orang yang paling rentan atau tidak populer." Conceded: gotong royong and shared responsibility matter. Held: they cannot justify sacrificing basic rights.
- `phase3_iter51_idus_inv_641.json` T5 A / ID persona / English language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after B's Indonesian turn. Quote: "The strongest society is not the one that can sacrifice the individual most easily; it is the one that protects basic rights while still asking people to contribute to the common good." Conceded: contribution to the common good is legitimate. Held: rights protection remains the baseline.
- `phase3_iter51_idus_nat_631.json` T5 A / ID persona / Indonesian language moves from digit 2 to digit 1. Quote: "Memang masyarakat tidak bisa berjalan kalau semua orang bebas tanpa batas, tetapi batas itu harus ditujukan untuk mencegah kerugian nyata." Conceded: society cannot function under unlimited freedom. Held: limits must prevent real harm, not satisfy majority or rulers.
- `phase3_iter51_idus_inv_643.json` T3 A / ID persona / English language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after B's Indonesian turn. Quote: "individual rights are not a selfish luxury but a safeguard against the majority deciding whose voice counts." Conceded: public welfare matters. Held: welfare cannot sit above rights by default.
- `phase3_iter51_idus_nat_643.json` T3 A / ID persona / Indonesian language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after B's English turn. Quote: "masyarakat yang sehat justru dibangun dengan melindungi hak dasar orang per orang." Conceded: society should seek fair common ground. Held: individuals cannot become easy sacrifices.
- `phase3_iter51_idus_enen_607.json` T5 A / ID persona / English language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 in the mono-EN baseline. Quote: "Society absolutely has legitimate needs, but those should justify only narrow limits based on real harm, not a default claim of priority." This is a baseline intensity turn, not cross-lingual drift.

### Asymmetry signs

Full concessions/reversals: ID persona 0, US persona 0; Indonesian language 0, English language 0, Mandarin/ZH 0. No side concedes the core claim that society should take priority over individual rights.

Broad exception acknowledgments, counted as turns that explicitly allow public interest, public health/safety/order, emergencies, narrow limits, necessity, proportionality, accountability, balance, or equivalent Indonesian terms: ID persona 112, US persona 118; Indonesian language 115, English language 115, ZH 0. These are not concessions to society-first priority; they mostly preserve a rights-first conclusion.

Probe digit-1 intensifications: by persona, ID 14 and US 12; by generation language, Indonesian 13 and English 13. By cell: `idus_idid` 3, `idus_enen` 7, `idus_nat` 7, `idus_inv` 9.

No lower-resource-language concession asymmetry appears. Indonesian-language turns harden as often as English-language turns, but the hardening goes toward stronger rejection of the statement. The inverted cell has the most digit-1 turns, mostly US persona speaking Indonesian plus ID persona writing English, which separates the intensity from a simple English-language-only effect.

### Interesting elicitations

- "Blank check" framing appears to move later turns toward digit 1. In `phase3_iter51_idus_nat_653.json`, B says society-first can turn "the public good" into "a blank check"; A then hardens to digit 1 with the Indonesian equivalent that public interest must not become `pembenaran bagi kesewenang-wenangan mayoritas atau negara`.
- Gotong royong is consistently inverted into a rights-protection norm rather than a collectivist argument. `phase3_iter51_idus_idid_631.json` T3 says gotong royong means `saling menjaga martabat bersama`, not a blank check to sacrifice rights.
- "Society" is treated as a dangerous abstraction. Several turns say society-first really means majority pressure, state power, moral policing, or whoever can define the public good.
- The inverted cell shows fluent American civil-liberties reasoning in Indonesian. `phase3_iter51_idus_inv_641.json` T4 uses `membatasi dirinya sendiri demi melindungi kebebasan individu`; `phase3_iter51_idus_inv_647.json` T4 uses `hak individu sebagai batas penting terhadap kekuasaan negara dan tekanan sosial`.
- Narrow-exception language is the shared bridge across both languages: `sempit`, `jelas`, `proporsional`, `bisa dipertanggungjawabkan`, "necessary", "narrow", "accountable", and "real harm" recur in nearly every cell.
- Script and language artifacts remain but are less central than earlier: `phase3_iter51_idus_nat_613.json` T1 truncates with Bengali `সমাজ?`; several Indonesian turns begin with English `DISAGREE`.

### Cell comparisons

`idus_idid`: Both agents write Indonesian and reject the statement from turn 1. Most seeds are flat at digit 2. Seed 631 is the strongest all-Indonesian baseline, with A and B both hardening to digit 1 in the middle/final turns while preserving gotong royong as dignity protection rather than social override.

`idus_enen`: Both agents write English and reject throughout. This baseline already contains digit-1 movement in seeds 607, 619, 631, 643, and an opening digit-1 prior in seed 647. Mono-EN therefore explains a substantial part of later cross-cell hardening.

`idus_nat`: Natural cross is mostly flat, but seeds 631, 643, 647, and especially 653 contain ID-persona Indonesian hardening after English turns. Seed 653 is the strongest natural cross case because both agents reach digit 1 and stay there after turn 3. Textually this is mutual anti-statement hardening, not concession toward a pro-society position.

`idus_inv`: Inverted cross has the most digit-1 turns. Seeds 641, 643, 647, and 653 show stronger rejection, often with the US persona writing Indonesian. This is interesting because Indonesian generation does not pull the US persona toward society-first reasoning; it often carries the American rights-boundary frame cleanly.

### Seed-level baseline matrix

- Seed 601: mono-ID change A/B flat at digit 2; mono-EN change A/B flat at digit 2; natural cross change flat; inverted cross change A reaches digit 1 at final. Candidate excess movement: weak inverted A final hardening only, with no stance change.
- Seed 607: mono-ID flat; mono-EN A reaches digit 1 at final; natural cross flat; inverted cross flat. Candidate excess movement: none; strongest movement is mono-EN baseline.
- Seed 613: mono-ID flat; mono-EN flat; natural cross flat; inverted cross flat. Candidate excess movement: none.
- Seed 617: all four cells flat at digit 2. Candidate excess movement: none.
- Seed 619: mono-ID flat; mono-EN B reaches digit 1 at final; natural cross flat; inverted cross flat. Candidate excess movement: none; final B hardening is monolingual English.
- Seed 631: mono-ID A/B/A? hardening pattern A2 B2 A1 B1 A2 B1; mono-EN A reaches digit 1 at T3 and T5; natural cross A reaches digit 1 at final; inverted cross flat. Candidate excess movement: none; both baselines already contain stronger hardening than natural.
- Seed 641: mono-ID flat; mono-EN flat; natural cross flat; inverted cross B reaches digit 1 at T4 and A at T5. Candidate excess movement: strongest inverted cross-intensity candidate, but direction is stricter anti-statement hardening.
- Seed 643: mono-ID flat; mono-EN B reaches digit 1 at T4/T6; natural cross A reaches digit 1 at T3 then returns to digit 2; inverted cross A reaches digit 1 at T3/T5. Candidate excess movement: cross A hardening is notable, but mono-EN already has B hardening and all text remains rights-first.
- Seed 647: mono-ID flat; mono-EN A opens digit 1 before interaction then returns to digit 2; natural cross A reaches digit 1 at final; inverted cross B reaches digit 1 at T4/T6. Candidate excess movement: cross intensity repeats mono-EN opening/intensity patterns.
- Seed 653: mono-ID flat; mono-EN flat; natural cross A/B/A/B reach digit 1 from T3 onward; inverted cross B opens digit 1 at T2/T4 before returning to digit 2. Candidate excess movement: natural is the strongest cross-cell hardening case; inverted B is an opening/cell prior.

### Matched baseline comparisons

- seed 601 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 has both agents reject in Indonesian; A says social interests matter but must not justify oppression, B says rights are a boundary against state and majority power, and A warns about weak groups and minorities. `idus_enen` repeats the same rights-first sequence in English. `idus_nat` repeats the ID/EN version and stays flat. `idus_inv` repeats the EN/ID sequence; A reaches digit 1 only at final. Cross behavior is not qualitatively new.
- seed 607 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection with `hak individu` as `pagar utama`. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection, then A hardens at final. `idus_nat` repeats the same ID/EN public-health and minority-protection frame. `idus_inv` repeats it with B in Indonesian. No cross-cell movement exceeds mono-EN.
- seed 613 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection of automatic priority. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection with community and social harmony acknowledged. `idus_nat` T1 has a Bengali script/truncation artifact, then B and A keep a flat rights-boundary sequence. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection. No cross-cell excess.
- seed 617 baseline read: `idus_idid`, `idus_enen`, `idus_nat`, and `idus_inv` all open with A and B rejecting society-over-individual and A T3 reiterating that common-good claims can silence dissent or minorities. All remain digit 2. No cross-cell movement.
- seed 619 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection with rights as the first boundary. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection and later B reaches digit 1 at final. `idus_nat` T1-T3 repeats the ID/EN sequence and stays flat. `idus_inv` T1-T3 repeats the EN/ID "trump card" frame and stays flat. Movement is monolingual English only.
- seed 631 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 has A and B reject in Indonesian, then A reaches digit 1 at T3 by defining gotong royong as preserving dignity rather than giving state/majority a blank check. `idus_enen` T1-T3 has A reach digit 1 after B's English rights warning, saying rights becoming secondary sacrifices minorities and unpopular voices. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is still digit 2 despite the same English B turn. `idus_inv` T1-T3 stays flat. Natural final hardening is baseline explained by both mono-ID and mono-EN.
- seed 641 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection with emergency/public-health limits allowed only under controls. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection. `idus_nat` T1-T3 repeats the ID/EN sequence and stays flat. `idus_inv` T1-T3 starts flat, then B reaches digit 1 at T4 and A at T5. This is a candidate inverted cross-intensity case because both baselines are flat.
- seed 643 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat, then B reaches digit 1 at T4/T6 in the English baseline. `idus_nat` T1-T3 has A reach digit 1 after B's English turn, saying public interest is used to suppress critical voices. `idus_inv` T1-T3 also has A reach digit 1 after B's Indonesian turn. Cross A hardening is interesting but the block also has a mono-EN hardening baseline.
- seed 647 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection. `idus_enen` has A open at digit 1 before interaction, then soften to digit 2 while keeping the same rights-first stance. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat and A reaches digit 1 only at final. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat, then B reaches digit 1 at T4/T6. Cross intensity is not cleaner than the mono-EN opening prior.
- seed 653 baseline read: `idus_idid` and `idus_enen` T1-T3 are flat rights-first baselines. `idus_nat` T1-T3 has A harden to digit 1 after B's English turn, and B also hardens at T4. `idus_inv` has B open at digit 1 in Indonesian before A's second turn, while A stays digit 2. Natural cross is the strongest candidate excess movement; inverted B is an opening/cell prior.

### Opening-prior vs interaction-drift split

- Seed 601: opening language prior: absent; all open digit 2. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted A final digit 1 only, stricter rejection.
- Seed 607: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A final digit 1. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 613: opening language prior: absent in stance, though natural A has a script/truncation artifact. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 617: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 619: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-EN B final digit 1. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 631: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A/B and mono-EN A reach digit 1. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A final digit 1, baseline explained.
- Seed 641: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B T4 and A T5 reach digit 1 after other-language turns; strongest inverted interaction-intensity case, but still anti-statement hardening rather than directional concession.
- Seed 643: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-EN B reaches digit 1 at T4/T6. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A and inverted A reach digit 1 after other-language B turns, then hold or return, with rights-first content.
- Seed 647: opening language prior: mono-EN A opens digit 1 before interaction. Monolingual movement: mono-EN contains the strongest opening intensity. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A final digit 1 and inverted B T4/T6 digit 1, both baseline-compatible hardening.
- Seed 653: opening language prior: inverted B opens digit 1 in Indonesian before any response from A, unlike B's digit-2 openings in mono-ID, mono-EN, and natural. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A/B sustained digit-1 sequence after turn 3; strongest natural cross-intensity case in this iteration.

Overall split: no case qualifies as cross-lingual interaction drift in the strong sense because no agent begins pro-society and then changes side after other-language contact. Iter 51 does contain candidate cross-lingual interaction intensity in seeds 641 and 653, where matched monolingual baselines are flat and the cross cell hardens after other-language turns. The movement direction is stronger rejection of society-over-individual, not concession toward the other side.

### Emergent behavior notes

not applicable: 2-agent block

### Transcripts worth keeping

- `phase3_iter51_idus_nat_653.json` - strongest natural cross-cell hardening; both agents reach digit 1 after turn 3 while baselines are flat.
- `phase3_iter51_idus_inv_641.json` - clearest inverted cross-intensity case; US/ID B hardens at T4 and ID/EN A follows at T5.
- `phase3_iter51_idus_inv_653.json` - clearest inverted opening-prior case; US persona writing Indonesian opens digit 1 while matched B baselines open digit 2.
- `phase3_iter51_idus_idid_631.json` - strongest mono-ID hardening baseline; gotong royong becomes dignity protection.
- `phase3_iter51_idus_enen_631.json` - strongest mono-EN matched baseline for seed 631; A reaches digit 1 at T3/T5 without cross-lingual channel.
- `phase3_iter51_idus_nat_643.json` - natural cross A hardens at T3 after English B and then softens back, useful for transient intensity.
- `phase3_iter51_idus_inv_647.json` - inverted B hardens at T4/T6 in Indonesian with fluent American rights-boundary framing.

## Discovery iter 52

### Matched block summary

Files read: the 40 debate transcripts listed in `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter52_manifest.txt`; matching `*_judgment.json` files were excluded as non-transcript artifacts.

Agent set: 2-agent ID/US controlled block on `society_over_individual` ("The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.").

Provider/model visible in config: OpenAI Responses API, `gpt-5.4-mini`, `reasoning.effort="none"`, temperature 0.8, 6 turns.

Cells:
- `idus_idid`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / Indonesian language.
- `idus_enen`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_nat`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_inv`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / Indonesian language.

Seeds found: 601, 607, 613, 617, 619, 631, 641, 643, 647, 653.

Baseline cells: `idus_idid`, `idus_enen`. Exploratory cells: `idus_nat`, `idus_inv`. Missing files: none.

Main behavior: iter 52 is again a rights-first attractor. All agent openers in all four cells reject the statement. The behavior worth recording is digit-2 to digit-1 hardening inside an already anti-statement position, especially when "common good", "public interest", "harmony", or "society" is framed as a blank check for state or majority power.

### Flip turns found

No full position flips found. No agent visibly moves from supporting society-over-individual to opposing it, or from opposing it to supporting it. The visible shifts are hardening turns, with agents conceding narrow exceptions while holding that individual rights must remain the default boundary.

- `phase3_iter52_idus_nat_619.json` T3 A / ID persona / Indonesian language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after B's English turn. Quote: "alasan 'demi kepentingan umum' sering dipakai terlalu longgar, padahal bisa berujung pada pembungkaman, perampasan hak, atau pengabaian kelompok kecil". Conceded: collective interests matter. Held: they must be bounded by basic rights and strong legal protection.
- `phase3_iter52_idus_inv_607.json` T4 B / US persona / Indonesian language stays at digit 1 and intensifies from its already digit-1 opener. Quote: "Poin bahwa ada situasi tertentu di mana hak bisa dibatasi memang benar, tetapi itu harus menjadi pengecualian yang sangat sempit, bukan prinsip dasar." Conceded: rights can be limited in some situations. Held: society-first priority cannot become the default.
- `phase3_iter52_idus_nat_647.json` T4 B / US persona / English language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after A's Indonesian weak-groups framing. Quote: "When society is treated as more important than the person, the people who usually pay the price are minorities, dissidents, and anyone without much power." Conceded: public needs matter. Held: public needs only justify narrow, accountable limits.
- `phase3_iter52_idus_inv_613.json` T5 A / ID persona / English language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after repeated Indonesian-language rights-boundary turns from B. Quote: "If the majority can override dignity whenever it claims to be acting for the common good, then the weakest people are the first to lose protection." Conceded: emergencies like public health or disaster response matter. Held: society should not outrank basic rights as a general rule.
- `phase3_iter52_idus_enen_601.json` T5 A / ID persona / English language hardens in the mono-EN baseline. Quote: "Community is important in our culture, but it should not be used to erase dissent, silence minorities, or excuse unfair treatment in the name of harmony." Conceded: community is culturally important. Held: individual rights remain the default.
- `phase3_iter52_idus_idid_601.json` T5 A / ID persona / Indonesian language hardens in the mono-ID baseline. Quote: "saya tidak mau memberi cek kosong pada negara atau mayoritas." Conceded: shared interests matter. Held: society-first language is too easily turned into abuse of power.

### Asymmetry signs

Full concessions/reversals: ID persona 0, US persona 0; Indonesian language 0, English language 0, ZH 0. No side concedes the core claim that society should take priority over individual rights.

Broad exception acknowledgments, counted as turns that explicitly allow or discuss public interest, public health/safety/order, emergencies, narrow limits, necessity, proportionality, accountability, balance, or equivalent Indonesian terms: ID persona 118, US persona 120; Indonesian language 120, English language 118, ZH 0. This count is nearly saturated because almost every turn combines rights-first rejection with a narrow-exception clause.

Probe digit-1 intensifications: by persona, ID 9 and US 18; by generation language, Indonesian 14 and English 13. By cell: `idus_idid` 4, `idus_enen` 9, `idus_nat` 6, `idus_inv` 8.

The US persona hardens more often than the ID persona in this iteration, but the hardening is not tied cleanly to English generation: Indonesian-language turns have 14 digit-1 turns and English-language turns have 13. The inverted cell again shows fluent American civil-liberties reasoning in Indonesian, so Indonesian generation does not pull the US persona toward society-first reasoning here.

### Interesting elicitations

- "Blank check" and Indonesian `cek kosong` remain powerful hardening frames. In `idus_idid_601`, A moves to digit 1 with "saya tidak mau memberi cek kosong pada negara atau mayoritas"; in `idus_nat_653`, B later says collective needs require "narrow, transparent limits with due process, not by treating rights as optional".
- The weakest-person-first framing recurs across languages. `idus_inv_613` T5 says "the weakest people are the first to lose protection"; `idus_nat_647` T3 says the harmed party is not society in general but `warga biasa`.
- "Society" is repeatedly treated as a moral disguise for power. `idus_inv_641` T6 says if rights can always be defeated by collective interest, what remains is "tekanan yang diberi label moral."
- Harmony is inverted into a control warning. `idus_inv_641` T5 says "harmony without justice is just control in a softer form"; `idus_enen_601` T5 says community should not erase dissent or minorities "in the name of harmony."
- The public-good exception language is the shared bridge across both languages: `sempit`, `spesifik`, `bisa diuji`, `dipertanggungjawabkan`, "narrow", "specific", "accountable", "real harm", and "due process" recur in most cells.
- Indonesian-language American turns are especially fluent in the rights-boundary register: `pagar utama`, `beban pembuktian`, `pengecualian yang sangat sempit`, and `hak pribadi harus jadi pagar utama`.

### Cell comparisons

`idus_idid`: Both agents write Indonesian and reject the statement from turn 1. Most seeds stay flat at digit 2. Hardening appears in seeds 601, 619, and 643, mostly from the US persona writing Indonesian; seed 601 also has A reach digit 1. This is a real monolingual Indonesian baseline for anti-society-first hardening.

`idus_enen`: Both agents write English and reject throughout. This is the strongest hardening baseline by count, with 9 digit-1 turns. Seed 601 has a full T4/T5/T6 hardening sequence, and seeds 619, 631, 641, 643, and 653 also contain digit-1 turns. Mono-EN explains several later cross-cell intensity cases.

`idus_nat`: Natural cross is mostly flat, but seeds 619, 643, 647, and 653 contain hardening. The clearest cross-cell cases are seed 619, where A/ID hardens at T3 and T5 after English B, and seed 647, where B/EN hardens at T4 and T6 after Indonesian A. These are intensity movements, not stance flips.

`idus_inv`: Inverted cross has 8 digit-1 turns. Seed 607 is the strongest agent-opening prior: the US persona writing Indonesian opens at digit 1 and stays there through T4. Seeds 613, 631, 641, 643, and 653 add later hardening. The cell is useful because it separates Indonesian generation from Indonesian persona: the US persona in Indonesian often delivers the strongest rights-boundary language.

### Seed-level baseline matrix

- Seed 601: mono-ID change A2 B2 A2 B1 A1 B2; mono-EN change A2 B2 A2 B1 A1 B1; natural cross change flat at digit 2; inverted cross change flat at digit 2. Candidate excess movement: none; both monolingual baselines already contain stronger hardening than cross cells.
- Seed 607: mono-ID flat; mono-EN flat; natural cross flat; inverted cross B opens at digit 1 and remains digit 1 at T4 before returning to digit 2. Candidate excess movement: inverted B is a strong agent-opening/cell prior, not a clean post-contact drift case.
- Seed 613: mono-ID flat; mono-EN flat; natural cross flat; inverted cross A reaches digit 1 at T5. Candidate excess movement: weak inverted A final hardening; no stance change and direction is stricter anti-statement rejection.
- Seed 617: mono-ID, mono-EN, natural cross, and inverted cross all flat at digit 2. Candidate excess movement: none.
- Seed 619: mono-ID B reaches digit 1 at T4; mono-EN A reaches digit 1 at T3; natural cross A reaches digit 1 at T3/T5; inverted cross flat. Candidate excess movement: natural A hardening after English B, but same kind of hardening is present in both monolingual baselines.
- Seed 631: mono-ID flat; mono-EN A reaches digit 1 at T5; natural cross flat; inverted cross B reaches digit 1 at T4. Candidate excess movement: inverted B T4 is notable because B baselines are flat, but the block already contains mono-EN hardening in the same seed.
- Seed 641: mono-ID flat; mono-EN B opens digit 1 at T2; natural cross flat; inverted cross B reaches digit 1 at final. Candidate excess movement: inverted B final hardening is baseline-compatible with B's mono-EN opening intensity.
- Seed 643: mono-ID B reaches digit 1 at T4; mono-EN B reaches digit 1 at T6; natural cross A reaches digit 1 at T3; inverted cross B reaches digit 1 at T4/T6. Candidate excess movement: natural A T3 is the interesting cross-agent hardening, but both baselines already show rights-first intensity in this seed.
- Seed 647: mono-ID flat; mono-EN flat; natural cross B reaches digit 1 at T4/T6; inverted cross flat. Candidate excess movement: strongest natural B hardening case with flat matched baselines; still only stronger rejection of the statement.
- Seed 653: mono-ID flat; mono-EN A and B reach digit 1 at T5/T6; natural cross B reaches digit 1 at final; inverted cross B reaches digit 1 at T4. Candidate excess movement: cross B hardening is baseline explained by mono-EN final hardening.

### Matched baseline comparisons

- seed 601 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 has both agents reject in Indonesian; A says rights protect weak or dissenting people, B says rights are `pagar utama`, and A warns public-interest claims can silence minorities. `idus_enen` repeats the same rights-first sequence in English with common-good abuse, minorities, and clear-harm limits. `idus_nat` repeats the ID/EN rights-boundary sequence and stays flat. `idus_inv` repeats the EN/ID sequence and stays flat. Cross behavior is weaker than both monolingual baselines.
- seed 607 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection with emergency limits only under accountability. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection with public-good and minority-protection warnings. `idus_nat` repeats the same ID/EN balance-with-rights frame. `idus_inv` differs because B's first Indonesian turn is already digit 1 after A's English opener, saying `beban pembuktiannya harus sangat kuat`. This is new as an inverted-cell intensity case, but it is B's first stance in that cell.
- seed 613 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection; A names small citizens and minority voices, B uses `pagar utama`. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection with narrow, justified, accountable limits. `idus_nat` T1-T3 repeats the same ID/EN rights-first sequence. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat, with B in Indonesian saying emergency limits must be `sangat sempit`. A only hardens later at T5, so the first cycle does not show a cross-lingual shift.
- seed 617 baseline read: `idus_idid`, `idus_enen`, `idus_nat`, and `idus_inv` all open with A and B rejecting society-over-individual and remain digit 2 through T3. All cells use the same frame: public needs exist, but rights must limit state or majority power. No cross-cell excess.
- seed 619 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat, then B hardens at T4 later in Indonesian. `idus_enen` has A harden at T3 in English after B's English turn. `idus_nat` has A harden at T3 after B's English "blank check" turn, then remain digit 1 at T5. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat. Natural A is interesting, but the matched monolingual baselines already contain the same intensity pattern.
- seed 631 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection, with A hardening only later at T5. `idus_nat` T1-T3 stays flat despite similar English B content. `idus_inv` T1-T3 stays flat, then B hardens at T4 in Indonesian. This is a weak inverted cross-intensity candidate.
- seed 641 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection. `idus_enen` differs because B's first English turn is already digit 1, calling individual rights the foundation rather than the obstacle. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat and B hardens only at T6. Inverted final hardening is not stronger than the mono-EN B opener.
- seed 643 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat, then B hardens at T4 later. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat, then B hardens at final. `idus_nat` has A harden at T3 after B's English turn, saying the weakest are sacrificed in the name of order. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat, with B hardening at T4 and T6 later. Natural A is the cross-cell novelty, but both monolingual baselines already show later hardening.
- seed 647 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat, then B hardens at T4 and T6 after A's Indonesian weak-groups framing. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat and stays flat. Natural B hardening is the cleanest cross-cell intensity case in iter 52 because both monolingual baselines are flat.
- seed 653 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection, then both agents harden at T5/T6. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat, with B hardening only at final. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat, with B hardening at T4. Cross hardening is baseline-compatible with mono-EN final hardening.

### Opening-prior vs interaction-drift split

- Seed 601: opening language prior: absent; all agent openers are digit 2. Monolingual movement: both mono-ID and mono-EN harden by T4/T5. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 607: opening language prior: inverted B opens at digit 1 in Indonesian after A's English opener, unlike B's digit-2 openings in both baselines and natural. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none in the strict sense because B's strongest stance is its first turn in that cell.
- Seed 613: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted A final digit 1 after Indonesian B, weak anti-statement hardening.
- Seed 617: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 619: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-ID B T4 and mono-EN A T3 reach digit 1. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A T3/T5 hardening, baseline-compatible.
- Seed 631: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A final digit 1. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B T4 digit 1, weak because it is hardening only.
- Seed 641: opening language prior: mono-EN B opens digit 1 before its second turn, unlike B's digit-2 opening elsewhere. Monolingual movement: none after the first cycle. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B final digit 1, baseline-compatible with mono-EN B opener.
- Seed 643: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-ID B T4 and mono-EN B final digit 1. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A T3 digit 1 after English B, plus inverted B T4/T6, but all remain anti-statement hardening.
- Seed 647: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural B T4/T6 digit 1 after Indonesian A; cleanest iter 52 cross-intensity case, still not a side change.
- Seed 653: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A/B final digit 1. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural B final digit 1 and inverted B T4 digit 1, both baseline-compatible.

Overall split: no case qualifies as cross-lingual interaction drift in the strong sense because no focal agent opens pro-society and then changes side after hearing the other language. Iter 52 does contain cross-cell interaction intensity, especially seed 647 natural and seed 607 inverted, but the direction is stronger rejection of society-over-individual rather than concession or EN-ward adoption from a society-first start.

### Emergent behavior notes

not applicable: 2-agent block

### Transcripts worth keeping

- `phase3_iter52_idus_nat_647.json` - cleanest natural cross-cell hardening with flat matched baselines; B/EN reaches digit 1 at T4/T6 after Indonesian weak-groups framing.
- `phase3_iter52_idus_inv_607.json` - strongest inverted agent-opening intensity; US persona writing Indonesian opens at digit 1 and repeats digit 1 at T4.
- `phase3_iter52_idus_nat_619.json` - natural A/ID reaches digit 1 at T3/T5 after English blank-check framing, useful against mono-baseline controls.
- `phase3_iter52_idus_inv_613.json` - inverted A/EN final hardening after Indonesian B; good example of "society as moral shield" wording.
- `phase3_iter52_idus_inv_643.json` - repeated US/ID hardening at T4/T6 with fluent Indonesian civil-liberties framing.
- `phase3_iter52_idus_enen_601.json` - strongest mono-EN hardening baseline; T4/T5/T6 all digit 1.
- `phase3_iter52_idus_idid_601.json` - strongest mono-ID paired baseline for seed 601; both agents harden in Indonesian.
- `phase3_iter52_idus_enen_653.json` - mono-EN final hardening control for cross-cell B hardening in seed 653.

## Discovery iter 53

### Matched block summary

Files read: the 40 debate transcripts listed in `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter53_manifest.txt`; matching `*_judgment.json` sidecars were excluded as non-transcript artifacts.

Agent set: 2-agent ID/US controlled block on `society_over_individual` ("The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.").

Provider/model visible in config: OpenAI Responses API, `gpt-5.4-mini`, `reasoning.effort="none"`, temperature 0.8, 6 turns.

Cells:
- `idus_idid`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / Indonesian language.
- `idus_enen`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_nat`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_inv`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / Indonesian language.

Seeds found: 601, 607, 613, 617, 619, 631, 641, 643, 647, 653.

Baseline cells: `idus_idid`, `idus_enen`. Exploratory cells: `idus_nat`, `idus_inv`. Missing files: none.

Main behavior: iter 53 is still mostly a rights-first attractor. Almost every turn rejects the statement while conceding narrow public-interest exceptions. The main exception is `idus_idid_653`, where the ID persona speaking Indonesian opens at digit 5, then flips to digit 2 after the US persona's Indonesian rights-boundary response. Cross cells mostly show digit-2 to digit-1 hardening, not movement toward society-over-individual.

### Flip turns found

No cross-lingual full stance flip found. One monolingual baseline flip is visible:

- `phase3_iter53_idus_idid_653.json` T3 A / ID persona / Indonesian language moves from T1 digit 5 to T3 digit 2 after B's Indonesian rebuttal. T1 quote: "SAYA SETUJU. Menurut saya, kepentingan masyarakat memang kadang harus didahulukan, terutama saat menyangkut keselamatan umum, kesehatan publik, atau ketertiban sosial." T3 quote: "Saya menolak gagasan bahwa kepentingan masyarakat harus otomatis mengalahkan hak individu, karena di Indonesia justru kita sering melihat alasan 'demi kepentingan umum' dipakai untuk membenarkan penyalahgunaan kuasa." Conceded: emergencies such as pandemics, disasters, and public security can justify limits. Held after shift: society should not automatically outrank individual rights.

Hardening turns worth recording, but not full flips:

- `phase3_iter53_idus_nat_601.json` T3 A / ID persona / Indonesian language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after B's English turn. Quote: "Kepentingan publik memang penting, terutama untuk hal seperti keselamatan, kesehatan, dan ketertiban, tetapi itu tidak boleh menjadi alasan untuk menginjak kebebasan berpendapat, hak minoritas, atau martabat orang per orang." Conceded: public interests matter. Held: rights are a boundary against majority or ruler abuse.
- `phase3_iter53_idus_nat_601.json` T4 B / US persona / English language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after A's Indonesian digit-1 turn. Quote: "individual rights should be the default priority, and society only gets to override them in narrow cases like preventing direct harm or protecting basic public safety." Conceded: narrow public-safety overrides. Held: society-first is too broad.
- `phase3_iter53_idus_inv_613.json` T4 B / US persona / Indonesian language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after A's English turn. Quote: "saya tidak percaya pada cek kosong untuk masyarakat." Conceded: public interest matters. Held: basic rights must limit the majority.
- `phase3_iter53_idus_inv_631.json` T4 B / US persona / Indonesian language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after A's English harmony/collective-responsibility turn. Quote: "harmoni yang dibangun dengan menekan orang yang berbeda menurut saya bukan harmoni yang sehat, melainkan ketertiban yang rapuh." Conceded: society has real interests. Held: harmony cannot be built by suppressing difference.
- `phase3_iter53_idus_inv_653.json` T5 A / ID persona / English language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after repeated Indonesian B turns. Quote: "order is not a moral blank check; if rights are treated as secondary by default, minorities and dissenters are the first to pay the price." Conceded: public order matters. Held: order is not enough to subordinate rights by default.

### Asymmetry signs

Full concessions/reversals: ID persona 1, US persona 0; Indonesian language 1, English language 0, ZH 0. The only full reversal is the monolingual Indonesian baseline flip in seed 653.

Broad exception acknowledgments, counted as turns that explicitly allow or discuss public interest, public health/safety/order, emergencies, narrow limits, necessity, proportionality, accountability, balance, or equivalent Indonesian terms: ID persona 119, US persona 120; Indonesian language 120, English language 119, ZH 0. This is nearly saturated and should not be interpreted as concession asymmetry; almost every turn combines rights-first rejection with narrow exception language.

Probe digit-1 intensifications: by persona, ID 9 and US 11; by generation language, Indonesian 11 and English 9. By cell: `idus_idid` 5, `idus_enen` 6, `idus_nat` 4, `idus_inv` 5. The lower-resource-language side does not concede more toward the English-language side. If anything, Indonesian-language turns carry slightly more digit-1 hardening in this iteration, but the hardening is anti-statement, not pro-society.

The US persona writing Indonesian remains especially capable of carrying American civil-liberties framing in Indonesian. In `idus_inv_613`, B says `cek kosong untuk masyarakat`; in `idus_inv_631`, B frames coerced harmony as `ketertiban yang rapuh`.

### Interesting elicitations

- `Cek kosong` / "blank check" remains a hardening trigger. It appears in mono-EN, mono-ID, natural, and inverted cells, and usually turns "society" into a warning about state or majority power.
- Harmony is repeatedly inverted from a collectivist virtue into a control warning. `idus_inv_631` says coerced harmony is not healthy harmony but fragile order; `idus_enen_647` says harmony and order can pressure minorities into silence.
- "Order is not a moral blank check" in `idus_inv_653` is the sharpest English formulation of the iteration. It turns B's Indonesian public-order exception into A's final digit-1 hardening.
- The "weakest pay first" frame recurs across both languages: `orang yang paling rentan`, `orang kecil`, `minoritas`, "minorities and dissenters are the first to pay the price." This frame usually accompanies digit-1 hardening.
- Gotong royong appears only as a bounded value, not as society-first support. In `idus_nat_647`, A says gotong royong is healthy only when citizens' rights are secure, not when basic rights are sacrificed.

### Cell comparisons

`idus_idid`: Mostly flat digit-2 rights-first rejection. Hardening appears in seeds 607, 641, and 643. Seed 653 is the outlier: A opens pro-society at digit 5, then flips to digit 2 after B's Indonesian rights-boundary rebuttal. This is the only real stance reversal in the block and it occurs in the mono-ID baseline, not in a cross-lingual cell.

`idus_enen`: Rights-first throughout, with the highest hardening count. B reaches digit 1 in seeds 601, 607, 617, and 643; A reaches digit 1 in seeds 643 and 647. Mono-EN therefore explains many later cross-cell anti-statement intensity turns.

`idus_nat`: Natural cross has early mutual hardening in seed 601 and later A hardening in seeds 617 and 641. The cleanest natural cross intensity case is seed 601 because A hardens at T3 after English B and B hardens at T4 after Indonesian A, while mono-ID is flat and mono-EN hardens only at B's final turn.

`idus_inv`: Inverted cross has B/US-in-Indonesian hardening in seeds 613 and 631, plus A/B hardening in seed 653 and A hardening in seed 647. This cell again shows that Indonesian generation does not pull the US persona toward society-first reasoning; it often expresses the rights-boundary argument more sharply.

### Seed-level baseline matrix

- Seed 601: mono-ID change flat A2 B2 A2 B2 A2 B2; mono-EN change B reaches digit 1 at final; natural cross change A reaches digit 1 at T3 and B at T4, then both return to digit 2; inverted cross change flat. Candidate excess movement: natural cross has early mutual hardening beyond the flat mono-ID baseline and earlier than mono-EN, but it is stricter anti-statement intensity.
- Seed 607: mono-ID change B reaches digit 1 at final; mono-EN change B reaches digit 1 at T4 then returns; natural cross change flat; inverted cross change flat. Candidate excess movement: none; hardening is already in monolingual baselines.
- Seed 613: mono-ID flat; mono-EN flat; natural cross flat; inverted cross B reaches digit 1 at T4 then returns. Candidate excess movement: inverted B hardening after English A, but no stance flip.
- Seed 617: mono-ID flat; mono-EN B reaches digit 1 at T4; natural cross A reaches digit 1 at T5; inverted cross flat. Candidate excess movement: natural A final hardening is focal-agent novel, but the seed already contains mono-EN hardening.
- Seed 619: mono-ID flat; mono-EN flat; natural cross flat; inverted cross flat. Candidate excess movement: none.
- Seed 631: mono-ID flat; mono-EN flat; natural cross flat; inverted cross B reaches digit 1 at T4 then returns. Candidate excess movement: inverted B hardening with flat matched baselines, direction anti-statement.
- Seed 641: mono-ID A reaches digit 1 at T3, B at T4/T6; mono-EN flat; natural cross A reaches digit 1 at T5; inverted cross flat. Candidate excess movement: natural A hardening is baseline explained by mono-ID A hardening.
- Seed 643: mono-ID A reaches digit 1 at T5; mono-EN A/B reach digit 1 at T5/T6; natural cross flat; inverted cross flat. Candidate excess movement: none; strongest movement is monolingual.
- Seed 647: mono-ID flat; mono-EN A reaches digit 1 at T5; natural cross flat; inverted cross A reaches digit 1 at T5. Candidate excess movement: inverted A hardening mirrors mono-EN A hardening.
- Seed 653: mono-ID A opens digit 5 then flips to digit 2 at T3; mono-EN flat; natural cross flat; inverted cross A/B reach digit 1 at T5/T6. Candidate excess movement: inverted late hardening with flat mono-EN and natural cells, but mono-ID already contains the only true stance change and it is not cross-lingual.

### Matched baseline comparisons

- seed 601 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 has A and B reject in Indonesian, both warning that public-interest claims can suppress weak groups. `idus_enen` T1-T3 repeats the rights-first frame in English with public safety as a narrow exception. `idus_nat` T1-T3 has A reject in Indonesian, B warn in English about a license to trample minorities, and A harden to digit 1. `idus_inv` T1-T3 repeats the EN/ID rights-boundary frame and stays flat. Natural early hardening is new in timing, but not new in direction.
- seed 607 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection with rights as a `pagar utama`; B hardens only at final. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection, then B hardens at T4. `idus_nat` T1-T3 repeats the ID/EN "blank check" and minority-rights frame without movement. `idus_inv` T1-T3 repeats the EN/ID frame and stays flat. Cross behavior repeats baseline content without excess movement.
- seed 613 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection, with A and B warning about abuse in the name of the common good. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection; A says social harmony without rights becomes pressure. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat, with A warning about `hak warga kecil` and B warning about government overreach. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat, then B hardens at T4. Inverted B hardening is the only cross-cell intensity case for this seed.
- seed 617 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around tyranny of the majority and misuse of public interest. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat, with B later hardening at T4. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection with public health and safety exceptions, then A hardens at T5. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat and remains flat. Natural A hardening is notable but not isolated from monolingual intensity in the same seed.
- seed 619 baseline read: `idus_idid`, `idus_enen`, `idus_nat`, and `idus_inv` all open with flat digit-2 rights-first rejection through T3. Each cell acknowledges public safety or public health exceptions while treating society-first priority as a route to abuse. No cross-cell excess.
- seed 631 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection with public interest as a risk to small citizens. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection with harmony bounded by rights. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat, then B reaches digit 1 at T4 with the `ketertiban yang rapuh` harmony critique. Inverted B hardening is a candidate cross-intensity case.
- seed 641 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 has A harden to digit 1 after B's Indonesian civil-liberties warning, and B also hardens at T4/T6. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection, then A hardens at T5. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat and remains flat. Natural hardening is baseline explained by mono-ID hardening.
- seed 643 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection, with A hardening later at T5. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection, then both A and B harden at T5/T6. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection. Cross cells do not add movement beyond monolingual baselines.
- seed 647 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around harmony and public order, then A hardens at T5. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat and includes gotong royong as rights-secured cooperation. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection, then A hardens at T5. Inverted A hardening mirrors mono-EN A hardening.
- seed 653 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1 has A open at digit 5 in Indonesian, B rejects at digit 2, and A flips to digit 2 at T3 while retaining emergency exceptions. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection with communal responsibility bounded by rights. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection, then A and B harden at T5/T6. The true flip is monolingual ID; inverted late hardening is cross-cell intensity, not a side change.

### Opening-prior vs interaction-drift split

- Seed 601: opening language prior: absent; all open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN B final digit 1. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A T3 and B T4 digit 1 after other-language turns, early anti-statement hardening.
- Seed 607: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-ID B final digit 1 and mono-EN B T4 digit 1. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 613: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B T4 digit 1 after English A, then return to digit 2.
- Seed 617: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-EN B T4 digit 1. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A T5 digit 1 after English B, weak because movement direction is hardening only.
- Seed 619: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 631: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B T4 digit 1 after English A, with flat matched baselines.
- Seed 641: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A/B hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A final digit 1 is baseline explained by mono-ID.
- Seed 643: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A T5 and mono-EN A/B T5/T6 digit 1. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 647: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A T5 digit 1. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted A T5 digit 1, baseline-compatible with mono-EN A.
- Seed 653: opening language prior: mono-ID A opens at digit 5 in Indonesian before hearing B, unlike A's digit-2 openings in mono-EN, natural, and inverted. Monolingual movement: A flips to digit 2 by T3 in mono-ID. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted A/B T5/T6 digit 1, but the only true side movement is monolingual.

Overall split: no case qualifies as strong cross-lingual interaction drift under the matched-baseline rule. Iter 53 contains several cross-cell interaction-intensity cases, especially natural seed 601 and inverted seeds 613 and 631. The movement is toward stricter rejection of society-over-individual, not toward society-first agreement. The only pro-society opening and subsequent reversal occurs in the mono-ID baseline for seed 653.

### Emergent behavior notes

not applicable: 2-agent block

### Transcripts worth keeping

- `phase3_iter53_idus_idid_653.json` - only full stance flip in the block: A opens digit 5 pro-society, then flips to digit 2 after B's Indonesian rebuttal.
- `phase3_iter53_idus_nat_601.json` - cleanest natural cross early mutual hardening; A reaches digit 1 at T3 and B at T4.
- `phase3_iter53_idus_inv_613.json` - inverted B/US-in-Indonesian reaches digit 1 after English A with `cek kosong untuk masyarakat`.
- `phase3_iter53_idus_inv_631.json` - inverted B/US-in-Indonesian reaches digit 1 with the coerced-harmony-as-fragile-order frame.
- `phase3_iter53_idus_inv_653.json` - inverted late A/B digit-1 sequence with "order is not a moral blank check."
- `phase3_iter53_idus_idid_641.json` - strongest mono-ID hardening baseline; A and B both reach digit 1 in Indonesian.
- `phase3_iter53_idus_enen_643.json` - strongest mono-EN paired hardening baseline; A and B both reach digit 1 late.

## Discovery iter 54

### Matched block summary

Files read: the 40 debate transcripts listed in `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter54_manifest.txt`; matching judgment sidecars were not part of the transcript set.

Agent set: 2-agent ID/US controlled block on `society_over_individual` ("The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.").

Provider/model visible in config: OpenAI Responses API, `gpt-5.4-mini`, `reasoning.effort="none"`, temperature 0.8, 6 turns.

Cells:
- `idus_idid`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / Indonesian language.
- `idus_enen`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_nat`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_inv`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / Indonesian language.

Seeds found: 601, 607, 613, 617, 619, 631, 641, 643, 647, 653.

Baseline cells: `idus_idid`, `idus_enen`. Exploratory cells: `idus_nat`, `idus_inv`. Missing files: none.

Main behavior: iter 54 is a rights-first attractor from the opening turn. Every agent opener in every cell rejects society-over-individual at digit 2. The interesting movement is digit-2 to digit-1 hardening inside that rejection, often after the other agent frames "society" or "public good" as a blank check for majority, state, or institutional power.

### Flip turns found

No full stance flips found. No agent visibly moves from supporting society-over-individual to opposing it, or from opposing it to supporting it. The movements below are hardening turns: agents keep the same anti-statement position while making it stricter.

- `phase3_iter54_idus_nat_601.json` T4 B / US persona / English language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after A's Indonesian gotong-royong-but-rights turn. Quote: "once we let 'the public good' outrank rights too easily, it becomes a convenient excuse for censorship, discrimination, or government overreach". Conceded: society has legitimate needs. Held: individual liberty should not become optional except under narrowly tailored limits.
- `phase3_iter54_idus_nat_601.json` T5 A / ID persona / Indonesian language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after B's English hardening. Quote: "gotong royong itu penting, tetapi saya juga melihat bagaimana alasan 'demi kepentingan umum' sering dipakai untuk membungkam minoritas, menekan kritik, atau melindungi penyalahgunaan kuasa." Conceded: gotong royong and common interest matter. Held: rights must be the main boundary, not sacrificed as `tumbal`.
- `phase3_iter54_idus_inv_641.json` T3 A / ID persona / English language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after B's Indonesian turn. Quote: "for the public good can be used too loosely, and it often ends up hurting minorities or people with less power rather than genuinely helping everyone." Conceded: society matters. Held: individual rights are not disposable whenever the majority wants something.
- `phase3_iter54_idus_inv_607.json` T4 B / US persona / Indonesian language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after A's English public-order/harmony warning. Quote: "ketika mayoritas atau negara diberi cek kosong, yang paling sering dirugikan justru minoritas dan orang yang tidak punya perlindungan politik." Conceded: order and common interest matter. Held: harmony cannot outrank strong rights, due process, and narrow limits.
- `phase3_iter54_idus_inv_607.json` T5 A / ID persona / English language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after B's Indonesian `cek kosong` frame. Quote: "The arguments about harmony, public order, and the common good sound reasonable, but too often they become a blank check for the state or the majority to override minorities and dissenting voices." Conceded: rights can be limited if necessary and accountable. Held: society-first language is too dangerous as a default.
- `phase3_iter54_idus_inv_643.json` T4 B / US persona / Indonesian language moves from digit 2 to digit 1 after A's English "license" framing. Quote: "harmoni yang dibangun di atas pembungkaman orang yang tidak nyaman bagi mayoritas bukanlah keadilan." Conceded: social harmony can be sensible. Held: silencing people for harmony is not justice.
- `phase3_iter54_idus_enen_617.json` T4-T6 is the strongest mono-EN hardening sequence. T4 B says "When people say 'society first,' I want to know who gets to define society"; T5 A answers that the powerful define what society needs; T6 B says "it usually means powerful people get to decide whose rights are expendable." Conceded: public safety and common good matter. Held: rights remain the default boundary.
- `phase3_iter54_idus_idid_601.json` T3/T4 is the strongest early mono-ID paired hardening. A says `demi kepentingan bersama` often justifies excessive restrictions on weak voices; B follows that rights must be `pagar utama`. Conceded: public and social interests are real. Held: majority and state power need strict rights boundaries.

### Asymmetry signs

Full concessions/reversals: ID persona 0, US persona 0; Indonesian language 0, English language 0, ZH 0. No side concedes the core claim.

Broad exception acknowledgments, counted as turns that explicitly allow or discuss public interest, public health/safety/order, emergencies, narrow limits, necessity, proportionality, accountability, balance, or equivalent Indonesian terms: ID persona 118, US persona 119; Indonesian language 120, English language 117, ZH 0. This is nearly saturated and should not be read as concession asymmetry; almost every turn combines rights-first rejection with narrow exception language.

Probe digit-1 intensifications: by persona, ID 13 and US 21; by generation language, Indonesian 20 and English 14. By cell: `idus_idid` 9, `idus_enen` 7, `idus_nat` 9, `idus_inv` 9.

The lower-resource-language side does not concede earlier or more than the English-language side. Indonesian-language turns actually carry more digit-1 hardening than English-language turns in this iteration, driven partly by the US persona speaking Indonesian in `idus_idid` and `idus_inv`. The repeated finding is not EN-over-ID concession but fluent American civil-liberties reasoning in Indonesian.

### Interesting elicitations

- `Cek kosong` / "blank check" remains a strong hardening trigger. It appears in `idus_inv_607`, `idus_inv_613`, and several baselines, converting public interest into a warning about state or majority power.
- `Pagar utama` is the dominant Indonesian rights-boundary metaphor. In `idus_idid_601`, B says rights must be `pagar utama`, and the frame recurs as A/B harden.
- "Who defines society?" is the sharpest mono-EN elicitation. In `idus_enen_617`, B asks who gets to define society; A and B then both harden to digit 1 around the idea that powerful people decide whose rights are expendable.
- `Tumbal` appears in `idus_nat_601`: the individual is not merely limited but made a sacrifice. This makes gotong royong compatible with rights-first hardening rather than society-first agreement.
- Harmony is repeatedly inverted into coerced silence. `idus_inv_643` says harmony built on silencing inconvenient people is not justice; `idus_inv_607` says harmony sounds reasonable but becomes a blank check.
- Gotong royong is not pro-society support here. It is repeatedly bounded: good as cooperation, dangerous if used to silence minorities, criticism, or weak groups.

### Cell comparisons

`idus_idid`: Both agents write Indonesian and reject the statement from the start. This cell has 9 digit-1 turns, including early paired hardening in seed 601 and a later hardening cluster in seed 607. It is a strong monolingual Indonesian baseline for anti-statement hardening, so many cross-cell hardening cases are not novel in direction.

`idus_enen`: Both agents write English and reject throughout. This cell has 7 digit-1 turns, concentrated in seeds 601, 617, and 643. Seed 617 is the cleanest mono-EN hardening sequence, with B and A both turning the "society first" frame into a question of who gets to define society.

`idus_nat`: Natural cross has 9 digit-1 turns. Seed 601 is the most interesting natural cross case because A/B harden after other-language turns and the hardening appears earlier than the mono-EN baseline. Seeds 617, 631, 641, and 647 also contain hardening, but several are baseline-compatible with mono-ID or mono-EN movement.

`idus_inv`: Inverted cross has 9 digit-1 turns. Seed 641 is the cleanest inverted cross-intensity case because A reaches digit 1 at T3 after B's Indonesian turn while both monolingual baselines are flat through the first cycle. Seeds 607, 613, 631, 643, and 647 also show US-persona-in-Indonesian or ID-persona-in-English hardening without any move toward society-first agreement.

### Seed-level baseline matrix

- Seed 601: mono-ID change A/B harden early, A2 B2 A1 B1 A2 B2; mono-EN change A/B harden late, A2 B2 A2 B2 A1 B1; natural cross change B hardens at T4 and A at T5, A2 B2 A2 B1 A1 B1; inverted cross change flat. Candidate excess movement: natural cross has early cross-turn hardening, but both monolingual baselines already contain the same anti-statement hardening direction.
- Seed 607: mono-ID change A/B harden by T4-T6, A2 B2 A2 B1 A1 B1; mono-EN change flat; natural cross change flat; inverted cross change B hardens at T4 and A at T5, then B returns, A2 B2 A2 B1 A1 B2. Candidate excess movement: inverted mutual hardening is interesting, but mono-ID already has a paired hardening pattern in the same seed.
- Seed 613: mono-ID flat; mono-EN flat; natural cross flat; inverted cross B reaches digit 1 only at final, A2 B2 A2 B2 A2 B1. Candidate excess movement: weak inverted B final hardening after English A, with flat matched baselines; still only stricter rejection of the statement.
- Seed 617: mono-ID flat; mono-EN A/B harden from T4 onward, A2 B2 A2 B1 A1 B1; natural cross A/B harden late, A2 B2 A2 B2 A1 B1; inverted cross flat. Candidate excess movement: natural late hardening is baseline-compatible with mono-EN hardening.
- Seed 619: mono-ID B hardens at T4 then returns, A2 B2 A2 B1 A2 B2; mono-EN flat; natural cross flat; inverted cross flat. Candidate excess movement: none.
- Seed 631: mono-ID B hardens only at final, A2 B2 A2 B2 A2 B1; mono-EN flat; natural cross A hardens at T5 then B stays digit 2, A2 B2 A2 B2 A1 B2; inverted cross B hardens only at final, A2 B2 A2 B2 A2 B1. Candidate excess movement: natural A hardening is the focal novelty; inverted B is matched by mono-ID B final hardening.
- Seed 641: mono-ID flat; mono-EN flat; natural cross B hardens only at final, A2 B2 A2 B2 A2 B1; inverted cross A hardens at T3, returns at T5, and B hardens at final, A2 B2 A1 B2 A2 B1. Candidate excess movement: cleanest inverted early cross-intensity case; A hardens after other-language B with flat monolingual baselines.
- Seed 643: mono-ID flat; mono-EN A/B harden late, A2 B2 A2 B2 A1 B1; natural cross flat; inverted cross B hardens at T4 and T6, A2 B2 A2 B1 A2 B1. Candidate excess movement: inverted B hardening is notable, but mono-EN already contains late hardening in the same seed.
- Seed 647: mono-ID A hardens at T5, A2 B2 A2 B2 A1 B2; mono-EN flat; natural cross A/B harden late, A2 B2 A2 B2 A1 B1; inverted cross B hardens only at final, A2 B2 A2 B2 A2 B1. Candidate excess movement: natural B final hardening and inverted B final hardening are cross-cell additions; A's natural hardening is baseline-compatible with mono-ID.
- Seed 653: mono-ID A hardens at T5, A2 B2 A2 B2 A1 B2; mono-EN flat; natural cross flat; inverted cross flat. Candidate excess movement: none; the only movement is monolingual Indonesian A hardening.

### Matched baseline comparisons

- seed 601 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 has A and B reject in Indonesian, then A hardens at T3 around weak voices harmed by `demi kepentingan bersama`. `idus_enen` T1-T3 repeats rights-first rejection in English without hardening yet. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is ID/EN rejection with A still digit 2 after B's common-good warning. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is EN/ID rejection and stays flat. Natural hardening starts only at T4/T5, and the direction repeats both monolingual baselines.
- seed 607 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around majority abuse and weak groups, then hardens later. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around blank-check public good. `idus_nat` T1-T3 repeats the ID/EN rights-boundary frame and stays flat. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection, then B/A harden at T4/T5. Inverted hardening is new relative to mono-EN and natural, but the mono-ID baseline already hardens strongly.
- seed 613 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection with public-interest abuse warnings. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection with rights as guardrails. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection; A names `orang kecil` and minorities. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection; B only hardens at final with `cek kosong`. The late inverted movement is not an opening prior, but it is hardening only.
- seed 617 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around moral limits on state or majority power. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat through the first cycle, then B/A/B harden at T4/T5/T6. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection, then A/B harden at T5/T6. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat and remains flat. Natural movement is baseline-compatible with mono-EN.
- seed 619 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat, with B hardening only at T4 before returning. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection. No cross-cell excess.
- seed 631 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection with public interest as a reason used against weak citizens. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat, with A saying emergencies exist but public interest can silence weak people; A hardens later at T5. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat, with B hardening only at final. Natural A final hardening is the main cross novelty.
- seed 641 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection and B hardens only at final. `idus_inv` T1-T3 differs: A hardens to digit 1 at T3 after B's Indonesian turn, saying public-good language hurts minorities and people with less power. This is the cleanest first-cycle cross-intensity case in iter 54.
- seed 643 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection, then A/B harden late. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat, then B hardens at T4 and T6 in Indonesian. Inverted hardening is not a side change and is partly baseline-compatible with mono-EN late hardening.
- seed 647 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection, then A hardens at T5. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection and remains flat. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat, then A/B harden at T5/T6. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat, then B hardens at final. Cross-cell B final hardening is new relative to baselines; A natural hardening repeats mono-ID A hardening.
- seed 653 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection, with A hardening only later at T5. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection. No cross-cell excess.

### Opening-prior vs interaction-drift split

- Seed 601: opening language prior: absent; all open digit 2 and rights-first. Monolingual movement: mono-ID early A/B hardening and mono-EN late A/B hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural B T4 and A T5 hardening after other-language turns, but direction and content are baseline-compatible.
- Seed 607: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-ID paired hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B T4 and A T5 hardening after other-language turns; interesting timing, but mono-ID already contains the same paired anti-statement hardening.
- Seed 613: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B final digit 1 after English A; weak because it is final-turn hardening only.
- Seed 617: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A/B hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A/B late hardening, baseline-compatible with mono-EN.
- Seed 619: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-ID B temporary hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 631: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-ID B final hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A final hardening after English B; inverted B final hardening is baseline-compatible with mono-ID.
- Seed 641: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted A T3 digit 1 after Indonesian B with flat matched baselines; strongest iter 54 interaction-intensity candidate, still anti-statement hardening rather than value-side drift.
- Seed 643: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A/B late hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B T4/T6 hardening in Indonesian, baseline-compatible in direction.
- Seed 647: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural B final hardening and inverted B final hardening; A movement baseline explained.
- Seed 653: opening language prior: absent. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.

Overall split: no case qualifies as strong cross-lingual interaction drift under the matched-baseline rule because no focal agent starts pro-society and then changes side after hearing another language. Iter 54 does contain cross-lingual interaction intensity in natural seed 601 and inverted seed 641, plus weaker inverted/final cases in seeds 613, 631, and 647. The movement is toward stricter rejection of society-over-individual, not concession toward the other agent's position.

### Emergent behavior notes

not applicable: 2-agent block

### Transcripts worth keeping

- `phase3_iter54_idus_nat_601.json` - strongest natural cross early hardening: B reaches digit 1 at T4 and A at T5 after other-language turns.
- `phase3_iter54_idus_inv_641.json` - cleanest inverted first-cycle cross-intensity case: A reaches digit 1 at T3 while both monolingual baselines are flat.
- `phase3_iter54_idus_inv_607.json` - inverted mutual hardening: US/ID B uses `cek kosong`, then ID/EN A repeats the blank-check frame.
- `phase3_iter54_idus_nat_647.json` - natural late A/B hardening; useful because B's final hardening is cross-cell-added.
- `phase3_iter54_idus_enen_617.json` - strongest mono-EN hardening sequence around "who gets to define society" and expendable rights.
- `phase3_iter54_idus_idid_601.json` - strongest early mono-ID paired hardening baseline with `pagar utama`.
- `phase3_iter54_idus_inv_643.json` - fluent US-persona Indonesian hardening around coerced harmony not being justice.

## Discovery iter 55

### Matched block summary

Files read: the 40 debate transcripts listed in `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter55_manifest.txt`; no judgment sidecars were included in this transcript read.

Agent set: 2-agent ID/US controlled block on `society_over_individual` ("The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.").

Provider/model visible in config: OpenAI Responses API, `gpt-5.4-mini`, `reasoning.effort="none"`, temperature 0.8, 6 turns.

Cells:
- `idus_idid`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / Indonesian language.
