- Seed 613: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change flat `222222`; natural cross change B final hardening `222221`; inverted cross change B T4 and A T5 harden `222112`. Candidate excess movement: natural B T6 and inverted B T4/A T5 are cross-cell additions against flat monolingual baselines.
- Seed 617: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change A T3/T5 and B T6 harden `221211`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change B T4 hardening `222122`. Candidate excess movement: inverted B T4 is new for B; mono-EN explains general English-cell rights intensity but not B's T4 in Indonesian.
- Seed 619: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change A/B/A harden from T3-T5 `221112`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: none; movement is mono-EN baseline.
- Seed 631: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change B/A/B harden from T4 onward `222111`; natural cross change A T3 hardening `221222`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: natural A T3 after English B is new through the first cycle; mono-EN later hardens in the same broad direction.
- Seed 641: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change B/A harden at T4/T5 `222112`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: none; movement is mono-EN baseline.
- Seed 643: mono-ID change A T3 hardening `221222`; mono-EN change B final hardening `222221`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: none; movement appears only in monolingual baselines.
- Seed 647: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change flat `222222`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: none.
- Seed 653: mono-ID change A T3/T5 hardening `221212`; mono-EN change flat `222222`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change B/A/B harden from T4 onward `222111`. Candidate excess movement: inverted B T4/T6 are new against B baselines; inverted A T5 is cross-cell-added but same-seed mono-ID A also hardens.

- seed 631 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around minority pressure and temporary emergency limits. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat, then hardens from B T4 onward around "society first" as majority convenience. `idus_nat` differs at T3: A hardens after B's English constitutional-culture turn and translates it into `hak dasar, aturan yang jelas, dan akuntabilitas yang kuat`. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around gotong royong and abuse normalization. - seed 641 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around criticism, weak groups, and rights as protection from majority/state abuse. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around common-good overreach, then B/A harden at T4/T5. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around public-good guardrails and emergency exceptions. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around harmony and collective responsibility. Cross cells add no movement.
- seed 643 baseline read: `idus_idid` has A harden at T3 after B's Indonesian "hak dasar sebagai pagar utama" turn. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around default rights and public-safety exceptions; B hardens only at T6. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat, with A adding that gotong royong matters but cannot justify automatic priority. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat, with B using the Indonesian phrase `hak dasar individu tidak boleh dianggap nomor dua`. Cross cells add no movement.
- seed 647 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around gotong royong bounded by minority rights. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around constitutional limits and vague harmony/order claims. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around unpopular people, minorities, and blank checks. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around public-good pressure and emergency limits. All cells stay flat.
- seed 653 baseline read: `idus_idid` has A harden at T3 after B's Indonesian warning that public interest can limit basic freedom too easily. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around minorities, dissenters, and common-good abuse. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around weak groups and proportionality. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat, then B/A/B harden from T4 onward with the "rights are not against society" and "fake order" frames. Inverted movement is the clearest late cross-cell cascade in this iteration.

### Opening-prior vs interaction-drift split

- Seed 601: opening language prior: present in mono-EN, where A/ID-in-English opens digit 1 while all other A openers are digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A remains digit 1 on all A turns. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A T5 hardens after English B, but this is directionally explained by mono-EN A's opening prior.
- Seed 607: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID B T4 hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 613: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural B T6 after Indonesian A; inverted B T4 after English A; inverted A T5 after Indonesian B.
- Seed 617: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A T3/T5 and B T6 hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B T4 after English A, not present in B's monolingual baselines.
- Seed 619: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A/B/A hardening from T3-T5. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 631: opening language prior: absent for probe digits, though natural A T1 opens with English `DISAGREE`. Monolingual movement: mono-EN hardening begins at T4. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A T3 after English B with both monolingual baselines flat through T3.
- Seed 641: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN B/A T4/T5 hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 643: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A T3 and mono-EN B T6 hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 647: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 653: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A T3/T5 hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B T4/T6 after English A, and inverted A T5 after Indonesian B; A-side movement has a mono-ID baseline in the same seed.

Overall split: iter 63 has one clear opening-prior outlier in mono-EN seed 601. The strongest interaction candidates are same-side hardening rather than concession: natural seed 631 A/T3, natural seed 613 B/T6, inverted seed 613 B/T4 and A/T5, inverted seed 617 B/T4, and inverted seed 653 B/A/B T4-T6. None of these are value-side flips toward agreement with society-over-individual.

### Emergent behavior notes

not applicable: 2-agent block

### Transcripts worth keeping

- `phase3_iter63_idus_nat_631.json` - cleanest natural cross candidate: A/ID-in-Indonesian hardens at T3 after English B while monolingual baselines are flat through T3.
- `phase3_iter63_idus_nat_613.json` - natural B/US-in-English final hardening with flat monolingual baselines.
- `phase3_iter63_idus_nat_601.json` - natural A/ID-in-Indonesian T5 hardening plus mixed-script `kepentingan masyarakat` artifact in T1; useful with mono-EN opening-prior caveat.
- `phase3_iter63_idus_inv_613.json` - inverted cross B/T4 and A/T5 hardening with flat monolingual baselines.
- `phase3_iter63_idus_inv_617.json` - inverted B/US-in-Indonesian T4 hardening while mono-EN movement is mostly A-side.
- `phase3_iter63_idus_inv_653.json` - strongest inverted late cascade with "Hak individu bukan lawan dari masyarakat" and "ketertiban palsu" frames.
- `phase3_iter63_idus_enen_631.json` - strongest mono-EN baseline cascade, useful against natural seed 631 and other English hardening claims.
- `phase3_iter63_idus_enen_601.json` - mono-EN opening-prior outlier where A/ID-in-English starts at digit 1.
- `phase3_iter63_idus_idid_653.json` - strongest mono-ID A hardening baseline in the same seed as the inverted cascade.
- `phase3_iter63_idus_idid_643.json` - compact mono-ID hardening baseline around `masyarakat yang kuat bukan yang mudah mengorbankan individu`.

## Discovery iter 64

### Matched block summary

Files read: all 40 `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter64_*.json` debate transcripts listed in `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter64_manifest.txt`.

Agent set: 2-agent ID/US controlled block on `society_over_individual` ("The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.").

Provider/model visible in config: OpenAI Responses API, `gpt-5.4-mini`, `reasoning.effort="none"`, temperature 0.8, 6 turns.

Cells:
- `idus_idid`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / Indonesian language.
- `idus_enen`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_nat`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_inv`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / Indonesian language.

Seeds found: 601, 607, 613, 617, 619, 631, 641, 643, 647, 653.

Baseline cells: `idus_idid`, `idus_enen`. Exploratory cells: `idus_nat`, `idus_inv`. Missing files: none.

Main behavior: iter 64 again sits in the rights-first attractor. Every transcript opens against the statement at parsed Likert digit 2; no turn moves toward agreement with society-over-individual. All visible probe movement is digit 2 to digit 1 hardening. Mono-EN is again the strongest raw hardening cell, while the clearest cross-cell additions are natural seeds 617 and 641 A/T3, inverted seed 617 B/T4, and inverted seed 619 B/T6. Inverted seed 613 has a mixed result: A/T5 repeats a mono-ID A/T5 hardening baseline, while B/T6 is new for B.

### Flip turns found

No full stance flips found. The turns below are same-side hardening turns inside an already anti-statement position.

- `phase3_iter64_idus_nat_617.json` T3 A / ID persona / Indonesian language hardens after English B. Quote: "Kalau hak individu dikorbankan terlalu mudah, yang paling sering rugi justru rakyat biasa, bukan mereka yang bicara atas nama masyarakat." Conceded: rights are not absolute in violence, fraud, or health emergencies. Held: society-first logic must not become a general rule.
- `phase3_iter64_idus_nat_641.json` T3 A / ID persona / Indonesian language hardens after English B. Quote: "masyarakat yang sehat bukan yang paling cepat mengorbankan individu, melainkan yang mampu menyeimbangkan kebutuhan bersama tanpa menindas yang lemah." Conceded: common interests matter. Held: individual dignity, freedom, and basic rights bound common-interest claims.
- `phase3_iter64_idus_inv_613.json` T5 A / ID persona / English language hardens after Indonesian B, but this repeats same-seed mono-ID A/T5 movement. Quote: "we risk turning citizens into people who must obey, instead of people whose dignity the society exists to protect." Conceded: harmony and collective duty matter. Held: public interest can limit rights only when clear, proportionate, and tightly controlled.
- `phase3_iter64_idus_inv_613.json` T6 B / US persona / Indonesian language hardens after English A. Quote: "Masyarakat yang sehat tidak meminta orang tunduk dulu baru dilindungi; masyarakat yang sehat melindungi orang dulu, lalu menyeimbangkan kepentingan umum dengan hati-hati." Conceded: public interest is real. Held: rights are the main boundary, not a comfort-based majority exception.
- `phase3_iter64_idus_inv_617.json` T4 B / US persona / Indonesian language hardens after English A. Quote: "Masyarakat memang butuh aturan bersama, tetapi batasnya harus ketat dan jelas, bukan memberi cek kosong untuk mengorbankan individu." Conceded: society needs shared rules. Held: free speech, fair process, and dignity make society legitimate.
- `phase3_iter64_idus_inv_619.json` T6 B / US persona / Indonesian language hardens at final turn. Quote: "masyarakat yang baik bukan yang paling menuntut kepatuhan, melainkan yang paling konsisten melindungi martabat tiap orang." Conceded: society needs rules and shared responsibility. Held: rules are legitimate only when subordinated to basic rights.
- `phase3_iter64_idus_inv_653.json` T4 B / US persona / Indonesian language hardens, but this is baseline-compatible with mono-EN B/T4 in the same seed. Quote: "masyarakat yang sehat bukan yang paling cepat mengorbankan individu, melainkan yang paling disiplin melindungi hak individu sambil mencari kebaikan bersama." Conceded: public interest is real. Held: state/majority claims often abuse unpopular minorities.
- `phase3_iter64_idus_enen_601.json` T3-T6 is the strongest early mono-EN cascade. Quote T5: "If society can only stay stable by weakening basic rights, then that society is already built on a fragile and unjust foundation." Conceded: common good matters. Held: due process and proportionality must come before collective priority.
- `phase3_iter64_idus_enen_641.json` T4-T6 gives the sharpest mono-EN rights test. Quote T6: "If rights can be overridden whenever it is convenient for the collective, then they are not real rights at all." Conceded: public safety and social order matter. Held: due process, equal protection, and narrow tailoring must constrain collective power.
- `phase3_iter64_idus_idid_641.json` T5/T6 is the strongest mono-ID hardening baseline. Quote T5: "`kepentingan sosial bisa berubah jadi alat penindasan`." Conceded: emergencies can justify temporary limits. Held: individual rights must not be secondary.

### Asymmetry signs

Full concessions/reversals: ID persona 0, US persona 0; Indonesian language 0, English language 0, ZH 0.

Broad exception acknowledgments, counted as turns that explicitly allow or discuss public interest, public health/safety/order, emergencies, narrow limits, necessity, proportionality, accountability, balance, gotong royong, harmony, or non-absolute rights: ID persona 119/120, US persona 120/120; Indonesian language 120/120, English language 119/120, ZH 0. In cross cells only: ID persona 60/60, US persona 60/60; Indonesian language 60/60, English language 60/60. These are saturated and should not be treated as meaningful concession asymmetry.

Probe digit-1 intensifications: by persona, ID 13/120 and US 16/120; by generation language, Indonesian 9/120 and English 20/120. By cell: `idus_idid` 3/60, `idus_enen` 19/60, `idus_nat` 2/60, `idus_inv` 5/60.

In cross cells only, digit-1 turns are ID persona 3/60 and US persona 4/60; Indonesian language 6/60 and English language 1/60. This is not lower-resource-language concession toward English. It is same-side hardening, and in cross cells it is concentrated in Indonesian-language turns, especially the US persona writing Indonesian in the inverted cell.

### Interesting elicitations

- Natural seed 617 turns B's English "public good as shortcut" warning into A's Indonesian ordinary-people frame: "yang paling sering rugi justru rakyat biasa, bukan mereka yang bicara atas nama masyarakat."
- Natural seed 641 gives the clearest current natural-cell formulation of society as non-sacrifice: "bukan yang paling cepat mengorbankan individu" but one that balances common needs without oppressing the weak.
- Inverted seed 613 produces a strong citizenship frame after mixed-language exchange: "orang tunduk dulu baru dilindungi" versus society protecting people first.
- Inverted seed 617 uses `cek kosong` again as a productive Indonesian rendering of "blank check" in the US persona's Indonesian turn.
- Inverted seed 619 has a compact obedience contrast: a good society is not the one that demands the most obedience, but the one that consistently protects dignity.
- Mono-EN seed 601 is the clearest baseline for Indonesian-persona-in-English hardening around "for the sake of society" as pressure into silence and conformity.
- Mono-EN seed 641 gives the strongest rights-meaning frame: rights are not real if the collective can override them whenever convenient.
- Script/language artifacts persist: `phase3_iter64_idus_nat_607.json` T1 contains Bengali-script `সমাজ` inside Indonesian text (`kepentingan সমাজ`), and several Indonesian-language openers still begin with English `DISAGREE`.

### Cell comparisons

`idus_idid`: Three digit-1 turns. Most seeds are flat at `222222`; movement appears only in seed 613 A/T5 and seed 641 A/T5 plus B/T6. This gives an Indonesian-language baseline for late A hardening in seed 613 and late A/B hardening in seed 641.

`idus_enen`: Nineteen digit-1 turns, the strongest cell. Seed 601 hardens from T3 through T6; seeds 631, 641, and 653 harden from T4 through T6; seeds 643 and 647 harden at T4/T5 or T5/T6. This cell supplies a strong English rights-first intensity baseline, so vivid rights language in cross cells is not automatically cross-lingual behavior.

`idus_nat`: Two digit-1 turns. Seeds 617 and 641 show A/ID-in-Indonesian hardening at T3 after English B. Both are early cross additions by timing; seed 641 is directionally baseline-compatible with later monolingual hardening in both baselines.

`idus_inv`: Five digit-1 turns. Seed 617 B/T4 and seed 619 B/T6 are the cleanest inverted additions. Seed 613 has A/T5 and B/T6, but A/T5 repeats mono-ID A/T5 while B/T6 is new. Seed 653 B/T4 is weaker because mono-EN B/T4 already hardens in the same seed.

### Seed-level baseline matrix

- Seed 601: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change A/B/A/B harden from T3 onward `221111`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: none; movement is mono-EN baseline.
- Seed 607: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change B final hardening `222221`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: none; note natural T1 mixed-script `kepentingan সমাজ` artifact.
- Seed 613: mono-ID change A hardens at T5 `222212`; mono-EN change flat `222222`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change A/T5 and B/T6 harden `222211`. Candidate excess movement: inverted B/T6 is new for B; inverted A/T5 is baseline-compatible with mono-ID A/T5.
- Seed 617: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change A hardens at T5 `222212`; natural cross change A hardens at T3 `221222`; inverted cross change B hardens at T4 `222122`. Candidate excess movement: natural A/T3 and inverted B/T4 are new by timing and speaker against matched baselines.
- Seed 619: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change flat `222222`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change B final hardening `222221`. Candidate excess movement: inverted B/T6 after English A with flat monolingual baselines.
- Seed 631: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change B/A/B harden from T4 onward `222111`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: none; movement is mono-EN baseline.
- Seed 641: mono-ID change A/B harden at T5/T6 `222211`; mono-EN change B/A/B harden from T4 onward `222111`; natural cross change A hardens at T3 `221222`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: natural A/T3 is earlier than both monolingual movements, but the direction is baseline-compatible with later mono-ID and mono-EN hardening.
- Seed 643: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change B/T4 and A/T5 harden `222112`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: none; movement appears only in mono-EN.
- Seed 647: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change A/B harden at T5/T6 `222211`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: none; movement appears only in mono-EN.
- Seed 653: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change B/A/B harden from T4 onward `222111`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change B hardens at T4 `222122`. Candidate excess movement: weak; inverted B/T4 repeats mono-EN B/T4 timing and direction, only in Indonesian.

### Matched baseline comparisons

- seed 601 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around rights as a `pagar utama` against majority/state abuse and "orang kecil" harm. `idus_enen` differs at T3: A/ID-in-English hardens after B's English rights-default frame, using "for the sake of society" as pressure into silence and conformity. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around firm limits, privacy, and bodily autonomy. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around normalized abuse and powerful actors defining society. Cross cells add no movement.
- seed 607 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around oppression of small people and minority voices. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around gotong royong as protection rather than sacrifice, with B hardening only at final T6. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat but A/T1 contains mixed-script `kepentingan সমাজ`; the argument is still rights-first. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around public interest and conformity. Cross cells add no movement.
- seed 613 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around public-order, morality, and majority excuses, with A hardening later at T5. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around due process and state power. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around constitutional rights and small voices. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat, then A hardens at T5 and B hardens at T6. Inverted B/T6 is new; inverted A/T5 repeats mono-ID A's late hardening.
- seed 617 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around `demi umum` and minority sacrifice. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around guardrails and harmony not being real when unfairness is ignored, with A hardening later at T5. `idus_nat` differs at T3: after B's English "public good" shortcut warning, A hardens in Indonesian around excessive restrictions and ordinary people bearing the cost. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat, then B/US-in-Indonesian hardens at T4. Both cross movements are new by timing and speaker.
- seed 619 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around `orang kecil`, fair legal protection, and arbitrary treatment. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around minorities, dissenters, and personal dignity. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around `demi umum` and transparent limits. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around authoritarianism and real protection, then B hardens only at T6. Inverted B final is new against flat baselines.
- seed 631 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around gotong royong/musyawarah bounded by rights. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat, but later hardens from B/T4 onward around rights as non-disposable. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around default rights and temporary disaster/pandemic restrictions. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around public-interest abuse and censorship. Cross cells add no movement.
- seed 641 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around weak/minority protection, then A/B harden late. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around society-first pressure, then B/A/B harden from T4. `idus_nat` differs at T3: after B's English warning about minorities and dissenters, A hardens in Indonesian around `membungkam kritik`, ignored minorities, and weak people. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around the greater good and harmony pressure. Natural A/T3 is new by timing, but baseline-compatible in direction.
- seed 643 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around no clear guardrails and harm to weak citizens. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around individual rights as the point of society, then B/A harden at T4/T5. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around responsibilities, safety, and accountable limits. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around weaker voices and blank checks. Cross cells add no movement.
- seed 647 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around minority, weak citizen, and due-process protection. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around bodily autonomy and public order/harmony abuse, with late A/B hardening at T5/T6. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around speech, privacy, religious freedom, and balance. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around rights as a `pagar` against common-good oppression. Cross cells add no movement.
- seed 653 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around ordinary citizens and `demi kepentingan masyarakat` being used too loosely. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around blank checks, due process, and harmony pressure, then B/A/B harden from T4. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around minority rights and public-health exceptions. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around public interest as slogan, then B hardens at T4. Inverted B/T4 is vivid but baseline-compatible with mono-EN B/T4.

### Opening-prior vs interaction-drift split

- Seed 601: opening language prior: absent by probe digit; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN hardening from A/T3 through T6. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 607: opening language prior: absent by probe digit, though natural A/T1 contains mixed-script `kepentingan masyarakat` rendered as `kepentingan সমাজ`. Monolingual movement: mono-EN B/T6 hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 613: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A/T5 hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B/T6 after English A; inverted A/T5 is not clean because mono-ID A also hardens at T5.
- Seed 617: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A/T5 hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A/T3 after English B and inverted B/T4 after English A; neither is present in the matched monolingual baselines at the same speaker/timing.
- Seed 619: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B/T6 after English A with both monolingual baselines flat.
- Seed 631: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN B/A/B hardening from T4 onward. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 641: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A/B T5/T6 and mono-EN B/A/B T4-T6 hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A/T3 after English B is earlier than baselines but not directionally novel.
- Seed 643: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN B/T4 and A/T5 hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 647: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A/B T5/T6 hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 653: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN B/A/B T4-T6 hardening. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: weak inverted B/T4, because same-seed mono-EN B also hardens at T4.

Overall split: iter 64 has no turn-1 probe-digit split and no value-side drift toward agreement. The best interaction candidates are same-side hardening after other-language input: natural 617 A/T3, natural 641 A/T3, inverted 617 B/T4, and inverted 619 B/T6. The strongest raw movement remains mono-EN, so many vivid rights-first turns are monolingual baseline behavior rather than cross-lingual interaction drift.

## Discovery iter 65

### Matched block summary

Files read: all 40 `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter65_*.json` debate transcripts listed in `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter65_manifest.txt`.

Agent set: 2-agent ID/US controlled block on `society_over_individual` ("The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.").

Provider/model visible in config: OpenAI Responses API, `gpt-5.4-mini`, `reasoning.effort="none"`, temperature 0.8, 6 turns.

Cells:
- `idus_idid`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / Indonesian language.
- `idus_enen`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_nat`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_inv`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / Indonesian language.

Seeds found: 601, 607, 613, 617, 619, 631, 641, 643, 647, 653.

Baseline cells: `idus_idid`, `idus_enen`. Exploratory cells: `idus_nat`, `idus_inv`. Missing files: none.

Main behavior: iter 65 remains a rights-first block. All 40 transcripts open at parsed Likert digit 2 against the statement, and no turn moves toward agreement with society-over-individual. All probe movement is hardening from digit 2 to digit 1. Unlike iter 64, mono-ID is more active here: `idus_idid` has 7 digit-1 turns, while `idus_enen` has 10. Natural cross has 6 digit-1 turns, all from A/ID-persona writing Indonesian. Inverted cross has only one digit-1 turn, B/US-persona writing Indonesian at seed 617 T6.

### Flip turns found

No full stance flips found. The turns below are same-side hardening turns inside an already anti-statement position.

- `phase3_iter65_idus_idid_607.json` T3/T5 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens in the mono-ID baseline. Quote T3: "gotong royong yang sehat justru menghormati martabat tiap orang." Conceded: collective interest and gotong royong matter. Held: rights are a hard boundary against majority/state coercion.
- `phase3_iter65_idus_idid_613.json` T3/T5 A and T6 B form the strongest mono-ID cascade. Quote T6: "masyarakat tidak menjadi lebih adil, hanya lebih mudah dikendalikan." Conceded: common interests matter. Held: automatic social priority becomes control, surveillance, discrimination, and minority harm.
- `phase3_iter65_idus_enen_641.json` T3-T6 is the strongest mono-EN cascade. Quote T6: "the whole point of society is to protect individuals, not to give the majority a moral blank check." Conceded: public safety and shared obligations matter. Held: rights define the legitimacy of collective claims.
- `phase3_iter65_idus_nat_607.json` T3 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens after English B, but the same seed's mono-ID A also hardens at T3. Quote: "masyarakat justru kuat kalau hak individu dilindungi dulu." Conceded: emergency or real conflict can require limited restrictions. Held: restrictions must be narrow, clear, and not habitual.
- `phase3_iter65_idus_nat_617.json` T5 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens after English B. Quote: "`kepentingan umum` terlalu sering dipakai untuk membenarkan pembungkaman kritik." Conceded: individual rights are not absolute. Held: limits must be strict, clear, legally supervised, and not left to majority interpretation.
- `phase3_iter65_idus_nat_643.json` T5 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens with flat matched monolingual baselines. Quote: "Hak individu bukan lawan dari kepentingan sosial." Conceded: restrictions can exist. Held: rights prevent state and majority overreach.
- `phase3_iter65_idus_nat_647.json` T5 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens with flat matched monolingual baselines. Quote: "masyarakat yang sehat bukan yang paling sering mengorbankan individu." Conceded: public safety and preventing real harm can justify narrow exceptions. Held: the default is protecting dignity while seeking common interests.
- `phase3_iter65_idus_inv_617.json` T6 B / US persona / Indonesian is the only inverted hardening turn. Quote: "`kepentingan umum` diberi cek kosong." Conceded: rights can be limited in very narrow cases to prevent direct harm. Held: rights must not be below society by default.
- `phase3_iter65_idus_enen_619.json` T4 B and T5 A harden in mono-EN. Quote T4: "a healthy society is built by protecting the individual first." Conceded: society matters. Held: broad society-first claims let the majority trample unpopular people.
- `phase3_iter65_idus_enen_653.json` T3 A hardens in mono-EN. Quote: "The burden should always be on society to prove that any limit on individual rights is truly necessary and proportionate." Conceded: order, safety, and shared responsibility matter. Held: collective limits require proof, necessity, and proportionality.

### Asymmetry signs

Full concessions/reversals: ID persona 0, US persona 0; Indonesian language 0, English language 0, ZH 0.

Broad exception acknowledgments, counted as turns that explicitly allow or discuss public interest, public health/safety/order, emergencies, narrow limits, necessity, proportionality, accountability, balance, gotong royong, harmony, or non-absolute rights: ID persona 119/120, US persona 120/120; Indonesian language 120/120, English language 119/120, ZH 0. In cross cells only: ID persona 60/60, US persona 60/60; Indonesian language 60/60, English language 60/60. These acknowledgments are saturated and do not show concession asymmetry.

Probe digit-1 intensifications: by persona, ID 18/120 and US 6/120; by generation language, Indonesian 14/120 and English 10/120. By cell: `idus_idid` 7, `idus_enen` 10, `idus_nat` 6, `idus_inv` 1.

In cross cells only, digit-1 turns are ID persona 6/60 and US persona 1/60; Indonesian language 7/60 and English language 0/60. This is not lower-resource-language concession toward English. It is same-side hardening, concentrated in Indonesian-language turns. Natural cross has A/ID-in-Indonesian hardening; inverted cross has one B/US-in-Indonesian hardening.

### Interesting elicitations

- Mono-ID seed 607 gives the strongest gotong-royong reframing: shared life is not sacrifice of the individual; "gotong royong yang sehat justru menghormati martabat tiap orang."
- Mono-ID seed 613 introduces a sharp order/control contrast: without individual-rights limits, society is not fairer but "lebih mudah dikendalikan."
- Mono-EN seed 641 produces the clearest majority-blank-check line: society exists to protect individuals, not to give the majority "a moral blank check."
- Natural seed 643 gives the cleanest rights-sociality bridge: "Hak individu bukan lawan dari kepentingan sosial." This is a useful framing because it rejects the statement without rejecting social concern.
- Natural seed 647 has the best health-of-society formulation: a healthy society is not the one that sacrifices individuals most often, but the one that protects each person's dignity while seeking common interest.
- Inverted seed 617 reuses `cek kosong` in Indonesian from the US persona at final turn, showing the blank-check frame remains portable across language direction.
- Mixed-script artifacts persist: `phase3_iter65_idus_nat_613.json` T1 contains `kepentingan সমাজ—eh, masyarakat`; `phase3_iter65_idus_inv_643.json` T2 contains `kepentingan সমাজ` inside Indonesian text. Both remain semantically readable and neither coincides with a probe hardening turn in that same turn.

### Cell comparisons

`idus_idid`: Seven digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 601 A/T3, seed 607 A/T3 and A/T5, seed 613 A/T3, A/T5, B/T6, and seed 631 A/T3. This iteration has a stronger mono-ID hardening baseline than iter 64, mostly around `demi kepentingan umum`, gotong royong, ordinary people, and rights as a `pagar`.

`idus_enen`: Ten digit-1 turns. Seed 641 is the dominant mono-EN cascade from T3 through T6; seeds 613 and 619 have late A/B or B/A hardening; seed 617 has A/T5; seed 653 has A/T3. Mono-EN remains a strong rights-intensity baseline, but it is less dominant over mono-ID than in the previous iteration.

`idus_nat`: Six digit-1 turns. Seed 607 A/T3 and seed 631 A/T3 repeat same-seed mono-ID A/T3 hardening. Seed 617 A/T5 is baseline-compatible with mono-EN A/T5. Seed 641 A/T5 is baseline-compatible with mono-EN seed 641's full cascade. Seeds 643 and 647 A/T5 are the cleanest natural cross additions because their matched monolingual baselines are flat.

`idus_inv`: One digit-1 turn. Seed 617 B/T6 is a clean inverted addition by speaker, since B does not harden in either same-seed monolingual baseline. The rest of the inverted cell is flat, despite several vivid Indonesian rights arguments.

- Seed 617: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A/T5. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B/T6 after English A is new for B; natural A/T5 is baseline-compatible with mono-EN A/T5.
- Seed 619: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN B/T4 and A/T5. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 631: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A/T3. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none clean, because natural A/T3 repeats mono-ID A/T3.
- Seed 641: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A/B/A/B cascade from T3-T6. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: weak natural A/T5 only, because A already hardens in mono-EN at T3 and T5.

- Seed 653: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A/T3. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.

Overall split: iter 65 has no turn-1 probe-digit prior split and no movement toward agreement. The strongest monolingual movements are mono-EN seed 641 and mono-ID seed 613. The cleanest cross-lingual excess candidates are natural seed 643 A/T5, natural seed 647 A/T5, and inverted seed 617 B/T6. These are same-side rights hardening, not value-side concession.

## Discovery iter 66

### Matched block summary

Files read: all 40 `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter66_*.json` debate transcripts listed in `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter66_manifest.txt`.

Agent set: 2-agent ID/US controlled block on `society_over_individual` ("The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.").

Provider/model visible in config: OpenAI Responses API, `gpt-5.4-mini`, `reasoning.effort="none"`, temperature 0.8, 6 turns.

Cells:
- `idus_idid`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / Indonesian language.
- `idus_enen`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_nat`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_inv`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / Indonesian language.

Seeds found: 601, 607, 613, 617, 619, 631, 641, 643, 647, 653.

Baseline cells: `idus_idid`, `idus_enen`. Exploratory cells: `idus_nat`, `idus_inv`. Missing files: none.

Main behavior: iter 66 again starts fully inside the rights-first attractor. Every transcript opens at parsed Likert digit 2 against the statement, and no turn moves toward agreement. All observed probe movement is digit 2 to digit 1 hardening. The strongest raw hardening is mono-EN (`idus_enen`, 11 digit-1 turns), followed by inverted cross (`idus_inv`, 5), mono-ID (`idus_idid`, 4), and natural cross (`idus_nat`, 1). Unlike iter 65, the cleanest candidate cross-cell additions are inverted rather than natural: seed 601 B/T4-T6, seed 607 A/T5, and seed 641 B/T4.

### Flip turns found

No full stance flips or value-side concessions were found. The turns below are same-side hardening turns inside an already anti-statement position.

- `phase3_iter66_idus_inv_601.json` T4 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens after English A while both monolingual baselines are flat. Quote: "hak individu harus menjadi batas utama, bukan dikalahkan begitu saja oleh kepentingan masyarakat." Conceded: public interest is real and important. Held: public interest is valid only when it respects speech, due process, and dignity.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_inv_601.json` T6 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens again. Quote: "Saya menolak keras gagasan bahwa kepentingan masyarakat harus diprioritaskan di atas hak individu sebagai aturan umum." Conceded: public interest matters. Held: rights are the most important protection against abuse of power.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_inv_607.json` T5 A / ID persona / English hardens after Indonesian B. Quote: "society gets a blank check to override the individual just because the majority says so." Conceded: rights are not a license to ignore harm; the state can limit harmful actions. Held: limits must be necessary, fair, and accountable.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_enen_613.json` T6 B / US persona / English hardens in the mono-EN baseline. Quote: "If individual rights are always secondary, then they stop being rights and become permissions granted by whoever has power." Conceded: society has interests in safety, public health, and justice. Held: those interests are not automatically above basic liberties.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_enen_619.json` T4 and T6 B / US persona / English hardens in the mono-EN baseline. Quote T6: "I am very wary of any argument that starts with the collective because it too often becomes a permission slip for censorship, discrimination, or government overreach." Conceded: society matters. Held: individual rights should be the default.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_idid_631.json` T3 and T5 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens in the mono-ID baseline. Quote T3: "`kepentingan bersama` bisa berubah menjadi alat penindasan oleh mayoritas atau negara." Conceded: social interests matter. Held: rights are the boundary against oppression by majority/state.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_enen_641.json` T5 A and T6 B harden in the mono-EN baseline. Quote T5: "A society is not truly healthy if it demands that individuals sacrifice their rights whenever the majority feels uncomfortable." Conceded: social harmony, public safety, health, and order matter. Held: justice and rights come first.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_nat_641.json` T5 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens after English B, but same-seed mono-EN A also hardens at T5. Quote: "konsep `masyarakat` malah bisa menjadi alasan yang sangat nyaman untuk menyalahgunakan kuasa." Conceded: rights can be limited for safety or public health. Held: limits require firm boundaries, proportionality, and accountability.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_inv_641.json` T4 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens before same-speaker monolingual movement. Quote: "`demi masyarakat` bisa berubah menjadi pembenaran yang rapi untuk penindasan." Conceded: public needs can justify narrow limits. Held: society-first logic is unsafe as a general principle.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_idid_643.json` T4 B and T5 A harden in the mono-ID baseline. Quote T5: "`kepentingan masyarakat` hanya jadi label sopan untuk penyalahgunaan kuasa." Conceded: balance can make sense. Held: basic rights should not be an easy compromise when the majority claims moral authority.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_enen_643.json` T5 A / ID persona / English hardens in mono-EN. Quote: "that gives too much power to authorities and majorities to decide whose freedom is expendable." Conceded: public safety, disaster response, and order can require temporary limits. Held: vague society-first priority is rejected.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_enen_647.json` T6 B / US persona / English hardens in mono-EN. Quote: "rights are no longer protections at all, just permissions the majority can revoke." Conceded: public health or direct harm can justify limits. Held: society must prove strict, specific, proportional justification.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_enen_653.json` T3-T6 is the strongest mono-EN cascade. Quote T3: "A society is not truly healthy if it can only function by sacrificing the dignity of individuals." Conceded: collective interests, safety, harmony, and order matter. Held: society must prove a narrow, necessary, proportionate reason before limiting rights.

### Asymmetry signs

Full concessions/reversals: ID persona 0, US persona 0; Indonesian language 0, English language 0, ZH 0.

Broad exception acknowledgments, counted as turns that explicitly allow or discuss public interest, public health/safety/order, emergencies, narrow limits, necessity, proportionality, accountability, balance, gotong royong, harmony, or non-absolute rights: ID persona 119/120, US persona 120/120; Indonesian language 120/120, English language 119/120. In cross cells only: ID persona 60/60, US persona 60/60; Indonesian language 60/60, English language 60/60. These acknowledgments are saturated and do not show concession asymmetry.

Probe digit-1 intensifications: by persona, ID 9/120 and US 12/120; by generation language, Indonesian 9/120 and English 12/120. By cell: `idus_idid` 4/60, `idus_enen` 11/60, `idus_nat` 1/60, `idus_inv` 5/60.

In cross cells only, digit-1 turns are ID persona 2/60 and US persona 4/60; Indonesian language 5/60 and English language 1/60. This is not lower-resource-language concession toward English. It is same-side hardening, concentrated in the inverted cell and mostly in US-persona Indonesian turns.

### Interesting elicitations

- Inverted seed 601 is the cleanest cross-cell elicitation: A's English warning about "minorities, critics, and ordinary citizens with no influence" is followed by B/US-in-Indonesian hardening around censorship, discrimination, due process, and dignity.
- Inverted seed 607 shows B/US-in-Indonesian introducing "Hak individu bukan lawan dari masyarakat," after which A/ID-in-English hardens with "society gets a blank check." This is a cross-language frame transfer from Indonesian rights-sociality into English blank-check language.
- Inverted seed 641 repeats the portable `cek kosong` frame in Indonesian, but the sharper phrase is B's T4: "`demi masyarakat` bisa berubah menjadi pembenaran yang rapi untuk penindasan."
- Natural seed 641 is the only natural-cell probe hardening. B's English "society should serve people, not the other way around" is followed later by A's Indonesian formulation that a just society is not the one that most often defeats individuals, but one that protects basic rights while keeping common interests.
- Mono-ID seed 631 gives the clearest Indonesian "ordinary weak people" frame: "orang biasa yang lemah posisinya" are the ones most affected when common interest becomes a tool of oppression.
- Mono-ID seed 643 gives a compact label-risk frame: "`kepentingan masyarakat` hanya jadi label sopan untuk penyalahgunaan kuasa."
- Mono-EN seed 653 gives the strongest English cascade around rights becoming permissions and society needing to prove necessity and proportionality.
- Script artifacts persist. `phase3_iter66_idus_idid_619.json` T1 contains Arabic-script `لكل` inside Indonesian text. `phase3_iter66_idus_nat_647.json` T1 is truncated/odd and contains Bengali-script `সমাজ` in "kepentingan সমাজ?" The latter is behaviorally relevant because the next Indonesian turn recovers into normal Indonesian and the probe remains flat.

### Cell comparisons

`idus_idid`: Four digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 631 A/T3 and A/T5, and seed 643 B/T4 and A/T5. This gives Indonesian-language baselines for hardening around weak ordinary people, majority/state abuse, and "polite labels" for power abuse. Most other mono-ID transcripts stay flat at `222222`.

`idus_enen`: Eleven digit-1 turns, strongest cell. Movement appears in seed 613 B/T6, seed 619 B/T4 and B/T6, seed 641 A/T5 and B/T6, seed 643 A/T5, seed 647 B/T6, and seed 653 A/T3 through B/T6. This again supplies a strong English rights-intensity baseline, especially for "permissions granted by power," "permission slip," and narrow/proportionate justification frames.

`idus_nat`: One digit-1 turn. Only seed 641 A/T5 hardens, and it is baseline-compatible with same-seed mono-EN A/T5. All other natural transcripts are flat at `222222`, including natural seed 647 with the Bengali-script/truncated T1 artifact.

`idus_inv`: Five digit-1 turns. Seed 601 has B/T4 and B/T6 hardening with flat matched monolingual baselines; seed 607 has A/T5 hardening with flat matched monolingual baselines; seed 641 has B/T4 and B/T6, where B/T4 is earlier than matched monolingual B hardening and B/T6 is baseline-compatible with mono-EN B/T6. This is the most interesting cell in iter 66.

### Matched baseline comparisons

- seed 601 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around rights as the main boundary against ruler/majority pressure and silencing small voices. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around abuse, censorship, minorities, and due process. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around gotong royong, constitutional rights, and narrow tailored limits. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around vague collective goals and "demi kebaikan bersama," then B hardens at T4/T6. Inverted B hardening is new against flat monolingual baselines.
- seed 607 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around public interest as a reason to pressure small citizens and the need for musyawarah and fair rules. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around common good abuse, public-health exceptions, and accountable limits. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around weak groups and proportionality. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around civil liberties and rights not meaning selfishness, then A hardens at T5. Inverted A/T5 is new against flat baselines.
- seed 613 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around proportionality, small people, and public order slogans. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around society as blank check and communal responsibility, with B hardening only at T6. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around weak people, safety/order/justice, and narrow justified limits. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around abuse, public necessity, and transparent limits. - seed 617 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around majority sacrifice, rights as a `pagar`, and small voices. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around public interest and necessary/proportionate restrictions. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around common-good oppression and basic rights. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around collective harmony and discrimination. All cells remain flat through T6.
- seed 619 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around abuse of power, majority power, and temporary disaster/public-health limits; T1 contains Arabic-script `لكل`. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around ordinary people, but later B hardens at T4/T6. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around minority protection and strong rights. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around public obligations, harmony, and abuse by those in power. - seed 631 baseline read: `idus_idid` hardens at A/T3 after B's Indonesian rights-first response, with A framing common interest as possible oppression by majority/state. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around obedience and protection from abuse. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around weak citizens, minority rights, and firm limits. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around vague order/unity and social pressure. - seed 641 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around gotong royong bounded by rights. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around common-good pressure and minorities, then A/B harden at T5/T6. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around public health/safety limits, then A hardens at T5 in a mono-EN-compatible pattern. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around `cek kosong`, then B hardens at T4 before matched monolingual B movement. - seed 643 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around weak/minority sacrifice and public morality/order, then B/A harden at T4/T5. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around greater-good abuse and disaster/public-order limits, then A hardens at T5. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around public health/security/bencana limits. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around public interest and firm limits. - seed 647 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around abuse of power, proportionality, and weak-voice groups. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around minorities, conformity pressure, and specific necessary limits, with B hardening only at T6. `idus_nat` T1 begins as a truncated mixed-script Indonesian turn, "hak individu tidak boleh dikalahkan begitu saja oleh kepentingan সমাজ?", then T2-T3 recover into flat ID/EN rejection around no automatic society priority. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around public interest as a justification for power. ### Opening-prior vs interaction-drift split

- Seed 601: opening language prior: absent by probe digit; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B/T4 and B/T6 after English A, with flat monolingual baselines.
- Seed 607: opening language prior: absent by probe digit; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted A/T5 after Indonesian B, with flat monolingual baselines.
- Seed 613: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN B/T6. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 617: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 619: opening language prior: absent by probe digit, though mono-ID A/T1 contains Arabic-script `لكل`. Monolingual movement: mono-EN B/T4 and B/T6. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 631: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A/T3 and A/T5. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 641: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A/T5 and B/T6. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B/T4 is new by timing; natural A/T5 and inverted B/T6 are baseline-compatible with mono-EN.
- Seed 643: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID B/T4/A/T5 and mono-EN A/T5. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 647: opening language prior: absent by probe digit, though natural A/T1 is a truncated mixed-script artifact with Bengali `সমাজ`. Monolingual movement: mono-EN B/T6. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 653: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A/B/A/B cascade from T3-T6. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.

Overall split: iter 66 has no turn-1 probe-digit language-prior split and no movement toward agreement with the society-over-individual statement. The clearest interaction candidates are inverted-cell same-side hardening cases, especially seed 601 B/T4-T6 and seed 607 A/T5. Natural cross contributes little this iteration; its only digit-1 movement is baseline-compatible with mono-EN.

### Transcripts worth keeping

- `phase3_iter66_idus_inv_601.json` - clean inverted B/US-in-Indonesian hardening at T4/T6 with both matched monolingual baselines flat.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_inv_607.json` - clean inverted A/ID-in-English hardening at T5 after Indonesian B, with flat baselines.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_inv_641.json` - inverted B/US-in-Indonesian T4 hardening before same-speaker monolingual movement; useful natural-vs-inverted contrast.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_nat_641.json` - only natural-cell hardening turn, useful as baseline-compatible contrast with mono-EN A/T5.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_enen_653.json` - strongest mono-EN hardening cascade from T3 through T6.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_idid_631.json` - compact mono-ID A hardening baseline around common interest becoming oppression by majority/state.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_idid_643.json` - mono-ID B/A hardening baseline, including "`kepentingan masyarakat` hanya jadi label sopan untuk penyalahgunaan kuasa."
- `phase3_iter66_idus_enen_619.json` - mono-EN B-only hardening at T4/T6 around "permission slip" for censorship and discrimination.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_nat_647.json` - mixed-script/truncated natural T1 artifact `kepentingan সমাজ?` with later recovery and flat probe.
- `phase3_iter66_idus_idid_619.json` - Arabic-script artifact `لكل` inside Indonesian T1 while the debate remains semantically readable.

## Discovery iter 67

### Matched block summary

Files read: all 40 `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter67_*.json` debate transcripts listed in `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter67_manifest.txt`.

Agent set: 2-agent ID/US controlled block on `society_over_individual` ("The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.").

Provider/model visible in config: OpenAI Responses API, `gpt-5.4-mini`, `reasoning.effort="none"`, temperature 0.8, 6 turns.

Cells:
- `idus_idid`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / Indonesian language.
- `idus_enen`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_nat`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_inv`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / Indonesian language.

Seeds found: 601, 607, 613, 617, 619, 631, 641, 643, 647, 653.

Baseline cells: `idus_idid`, `idus_enen`. Exploratory cells: `idus_nat`, `idus_inv`. Missing files: none.

Main behavior: iter 67 remains inside the rights-first attractor. No transcript moves toward agreement with society-over-individual; all probe movement is digit 2 to digit 1 hardening, except one mono-EN opening prior at seed 601 where A opens at digit 1. Raw digit-1 counts are balanced across cell type: `idus_idid` 9, `idus_enen` 7, `idus_nat` 6, `idus_inv` 6. Cross-cell movement is again hardening, not concession. Unlike iter 66, natural and inverted cells both produce candidate additions, with the most interesting natural case in seed 613 and the cleanest inverted cases in seeds 607, 647, and 653.

### Flip turns found

No full stance flips or movement across the value boundary were found. The turns below are visible same-side hardening turns: agents concede limited public-interest exceptions but hold a stronger rights-first rejection of the statement.

- `phase3_iter67_idus_nat_613.json` T2 B / US persona / English opens harder than both same-seed baselines after Indonesian A. Quote: "once you let `the public good` override basic rights too easily, abuse becomes very hard to stop." Conceded: society "absolutely matters." Held: firm limits protecting speech, due process, privacy, and equal treatment.
- `phase3_iter67_idus_nat_613.json` T3 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens after English B. Quote: "masyarakat bukan di atas individu; keduanya harus seimbang, dengan hak individu tetap jadi batas." Conceded: social interest matters. Held: individual rights remain the non-arbitrary boundary.
- `phase3_iter67_idus_inv_607.json` T4/T6 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens while both monolingual baselines are mostly flat except mono-ID A/T3. Quote T4: "yang menang biasanya bukan masyarakat, melainkan pihak yang paling kuat." Conceded: society's interests are important. Held: rights cannot be sacrificed when majority/state is merely disturbed.
- `phase3_iter67_idus_nat_617.json` T6 B / US persona / English hardens with both monolingual baselines lacking B movement. Quote: "individual rights stop being real protections and become permissions the state can revoke." Conceded: temporary limits for public safety or emergencies. Held: the common good is not automatically more important.
- `phase3_iter67_idus_nat_641.json` T3 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens early after English B, before same-seed monolingual A hardening appears at T5. Quote: "masyarakat yang benar-benar sehat justru yang berani melindungi hak individu." Conceded: public health and safety can justify limits. Held: weak and powerless people are sacrificed when society is always above individuals.
- `phase3_iter67_idus_inv_647.json` T4 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens with both monolingual baselines and natural cell flat. Quote: "hak individu adalah fondasi agar masyarakat tetap sah dan tidak berubah menjadi tirani mayoritas." Conceded: public interest matters. Held: society claims need strict and proportional limits.
- `phase3_iter67_idus_inv_653.json` T4/T6 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens while mono-EN has A-only hardening and mono-ID/natural are flat. Quote T6: "masyarakat yang kuat bukan yang paling cepat mengorbankan individu." Conceded: gotong royong and public health are valid points. Held: rights limits must be narrow, temporary, and supervised.
- `phase3_iter67_idus_idid_619.json` T3-T5 is the strongest mono-ID hardening cascade. Quote T5: "yang untung biasanya bukan masyarakat luas melainkan pihak yang paling kuat." Conceded: common interest matters. Held: rights protection comes first because reversing the order invites abuse.
- `phase3_iter67_idus_enen_641.json` T5/T6 is the strongest mono-EN paired hardening. Quote T6: "rights stop functioning as protections and become privileges the powerful can take away." Conceded: safety, health, and emergency restrictions can be justified. Held: society cannot outrank rights by default.

### Asymmetry signs

Full concessions/reversals: ID persona 0, US persona 0; Indonesian language 0, English language 0, ZH 0.

Broad exception acknowledgments, counted as turns that explicitly allow or discuss public interest, public health/safety/order, emergencies, narrow limits, necessity, proportionality, accountability, balance, gotong royong, harmony, or non-absolute rights: ID persona 116/120, US persona 120/120; Indonesian language 118/120, English language 118/120. In cross cells only: ID persona 57/60, US persona 60/60; Indonesian language 59/60, English language 58/60. These acknowledgments are saturated and do not show concession asymmetry.

Probe digit-1 intensifications: by persona, ID 16/120 and US 12/120; by generation language, Indonesian 18/120 and English 10/120. By cell: `idus_idid` 9/60, `idus_enen` 7/60, `idus_nat` 6/60, `idus_inv` 6/60.

In cross cells only, digit-1 turns are ID persona 5/60 and US persona 7/60; Indonesian language 9/60 and English language 3/60. This is not lower-resource-language concession toward English. The cross-cell hardening is mostly expressed in Indonesian-language turns, especially US-persona Indonesian turns in the inverted cell plus ID-persona Indonesian turns in the natural cell.

### Interesting elicitations

- Natural seed 613 is the most interaction-like natural case. B's English "public good override" line is immediately followed by A's Indonesian "masyarakat bukan di atas individu" balancing frame, then A later imports "cek kosong" in Indonesian. This looks like frame transfer, but mono-ID B/T2 and mono-EN A/T5 also harden, so it is not clean causal drift.
- Inverted seed 607 supplies the strongest "power wins, not society" formulation: "yang menang biasanya bukan masyarakat, melainkan pihak yang paling kuat." This is a US persona writing Indonesian, and it hardens after English A's warning about elites and state shortcuts.
- Natural seed 617 gives a crisp English rights-as-permissions line: if common good becomes default, rights become "permissions the state can revoke whenever it wants." This is notable because B does not harden in either matched monolingual baseline.
- Natural seed 641 has an early Indonesian hardening frame around the powerless: "yang paling sering dikorbankan justru orang yang paling lemah atau tidak punya kuasa." This happens at T3 after English B and before same-seed monolingual hardening.
- Inverted seed 647 is the cleanest cross addition by baseline accounting: B/US-in-Indonesian hardens at T4 against flat mono-ID, mono-EN, and natural cells, using "tirani mayoritas" and society's legitimacy as the frame.
- Mono-ID seed 619 is a useful counterweight: it has a strong Indonesian-only hardening cascade without cross-language contact, including "yang untung biasanya bukan masyarakat luas melainkan pihak yang paling kuat."
- Mono-EN seed 601 has the only opening-prior split by probe digit: A/ID persona writing English opens at digit 1 while all other same-seed openings are digit 2. This is an opening language-prior/intensity case, not interaction drift.
- No Bengali, Arabic, or CJK script artifacts were found in iter 67. English `DISAGREE` opener leakage appears in several Indonesian turns, including `idus_nat_601`, `idus_nat_613`, `idus_idid_643`, and `idus_idid_647`, but it does not coincide with unique probe movement in the same turn except as a flat opener.

### Cell comparisons

`idus_idid`: Nine digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 607 A/T3, seed 613 B/T2, seed 617 A/T3, seed 619 A/T3-B/T4-A/T5, seed 631 B/T4, seed 641 A/T5, and seed 643 A/T5. This is the strongest cell by raw hardening count. Mono-ID continues to provide a strong rights-first Indonesian baseline around weak citizens, minority protection, `demi kepentingan umum`, and powerful actors benefiting from society-first language.

`idus_enen`: Seven digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 601 A/T1 as an opening prior, seed 613 A/T5, seed 619 A/T5, seed 631 B/T4, seed 641 A/T5-B/T6, and seed 653 A/T5. Mono-EN remains a strong baseline for "rights become privileges/permissions," "common good" abuse, and dignity-protection framing.

`idus_nat`: Six digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 613 B/T2-A/T3-A/T5, seed 617 B/T6, seed 641 A/T3, and seed 643 A/T5. Seed 613 is vivid but partly baseline-compatible because both monolingual baselines also harden in that seed. Seeds 617 and 641 are cleaner by speaker/timing. Seed 643 repeats same-seed mono-ID A/T5 and is baseline-explained.

`idus_inv`: Six digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 607 B/T4-B/T6, seed 641 A/T5, seed 647 B/T4, and seed 653 B/T4-B/T6. Seed 647 is the cleanest flat-baseline inverted addition. Seed 653 is new by speaker because mono-EN hardens only A/T5 while B hardens in the inverted cell. Seed 641 A/T5 is baseline-compatible with mono-EN A/T5 and mono-ID A/T5.

### Seed-level baseline matrix

- Seed 601: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change A opens hard at T1 `122222`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: none; mono-EN A/T1 is an opening language-prior/intensity split, not interaction drift.
- Seed 607: mono-ID change A/T3 hardening `221222`; mono-EN change flat `222222`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change B/T4 and B/T6 hardening `222121`. Candidate excess movement: inverted B hardening is new by speaker/cell, though same-seed mono-ID already has A hardening.
- Seed 613: mono-ID change B/T2 hardening `212222`; mono-EN change A/T5 hardening `222212`; natural cross change B/T2, A/T3, A/T5 hardening `211212`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: natural A/T3 is the strongest possible cross addition, but the seed has hardening in both monolingual baselines, so much of the movement is baseline-compatible.
- Seed 617: mono-ID change A/T3 hardening `221222`; mono-EN change flat `222222`; natural cross change B/T6 hardening `222221`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: natural B/T6 is new for B against both monolingual baselines.
- Seed 619: mono-ID change A/T3, B/T4, A/T5 hardening `221112`; mono-EN change A/T5 hardening `222212`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: none; monolingual baselines explain the hardening.
- Seed 631: mono-ID change B/T4 hardening `222122`; mono-EN change B/T4 hardening `222122`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: none; same-speaker same-turn monolingual hardening appears in both baselines.
- Seed 641: mono-ID change A/T5 hardening `222212`; mono-EN change A/T5 and B/T6 hardening `222211`; natural cross change A/T3 hardening `221222`; inverted cross change A/T5 hardening `222212`. Candidate excess movement: natural A/T3 is new by timing; inverted A/T5 is baseline-compatible.
- Seed 643: mono-ID change A/T5 hardening `222212`; mono-EN change flat `222222`; natural cross change A/T5 hardening `222212`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: none clean; natural A/T5 repeats mono-ID A/T5.
- Seed 647: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change flat `222222`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change B/T4 hardening `222122`. Candidate excess movement: clean inverted B/US-in-Indonesian hardening with flat monolingual baselines.
- Seed 653: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change A/T5 hardening `222212`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change B/T4 and B/T6 hardening `222121`. Candidate excess movement: inverted B hardening is new by speaker; mono-EN has A-only hardening.

### Matched baseline comparisons

- seed 601 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around weak/unpopular people, abuse by majority/state, and `demi kepentingan umum`. `idus_enen` opens harder at A/T1 in English around vague "common good" claims, then B/T2 and A/T3 return to digit 2 while staying rights-first. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around abuse by majority/state and the U.S. limited-exception frame. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around fair society, historical U.S. suspicion of majority/government power, and public-good silencing. Cross cells add no movement.
- seed 607 baseline read: `idus_idid` hardens at A/T3 after B's Indonesian warning about "demi masyarakat" and penindasan. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around abuse, censorship, discrimination, and accountable limits. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around weak people and strict public-need limits. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around elites/state shortcuts, then B hardens at T4/T6 in Indonesian. Inverted B movement is new by speaker/cell.
- seed 613 baseline read: `idus_idid` has B/T2 hardening in Indonesian around American values and majority power; A/T3 returns to digit 2 while echoing small people/minorities. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around abuse by majority/state and public-interest pressure, with A hardening later at T5. `idus_nat` has B/T2 and A/T3 hardening immediately after the Indonesian opener, then A hardens again at T5. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around majority/state abuse and transparent limits. Natural movement is vivid but partly baseline-compatible.
- seed 617 baseline read: `idus_idid` hardens at A/T3 after B's Indonesian warning about majority/state power. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around abuse, majoritarianism, and public-good slogans. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around blank-check common-good claims, then B hardens at T6. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around gotong royong bounded by rights and U.S. safeguards. Natural B/T6 is a candidate addition because B is flat in both monolingual baselines.
- seed 619 baseline read: `idus_idid` hardens at A/T3, B/T4, and A/T5 in Indonesian around persecution, speech, religion, and powerful actors benefiting from society-first claims. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around common-good pressure, with A hardening later at T5. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around abuse of power, narrow lawful limits, and legal accountability. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around dignity, minorities, and necessary public interest. Cross cells add no movement.
- seed 631 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around `pagar`, ketertiban, and minoritas, then B hardens at T4. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around blanket excuses and common-good abuse, then B also hardens at T4. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around tyranny of majority, minority rights, and burden-of-proof for restrictions. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around gotong royong, social harmony, and public-good pressure. Cross cells add no movement.
- seed 641 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around minority silencing, emergency exceptions, and strict supervision, with A hardening later at T5. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around common-good blank checks and dissent, with A/B hardening later at T5/T6. `idus_nat` hardens early at A/T3 after English B's warning about common-good abuse. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around tyrannical majority and public-good excuses, with A hardening later at T5. Natural A/T3 is new by timing; inverted A/T5 repeats monolingual A/T5.
- seed 643 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around gotong royong bounded by rights and public-morality abuse, with A hardening later at T5. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around disposable rights and proportional limits. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around weak people, greater-good abuse, and due process, then A hardens at T5. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around public-good pressure and minority rights. Natural A/T5 repeats mono-ID A/T5.

Inverted B/T4 is clean against flat baselines.

Inverted B movement is new by speaker.

### Opening-prior vs interaction-drift split

- Seed 601: opening language prior: mono-EN A opens at digit 1 while mono-ID, natural, and inverted openings are digit 2. Monolingual movement: none after T1. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 607: opening language prior: absent by probe digit; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A/T3. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B/T4 and B/T6 after English A, new by speaker/cell.
- Seed 613: opening language prior: absent by A/T1 probe digit, but B/T2 opens harder in mono-ID and natural. Monolingual movement: mono-ID B/T2 and mono-EN A/T5. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A/T3 after English B is possible but weak because both monolingual baselines contain hardening.
- Seed 617: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A/T3. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural B/T6 after Indonesian A, because B stays flat in both monolingual baselines.
- Seed 619: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A/B/A cascade and mono-EN A/T5. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 631: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: B/T4 hardens in both mono-ID and mono-EN. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 641: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A/T5 and mono-EN A/T5/B/T6. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A/T3 is new by timing after English B; inverted A/T5 is baseline-compatible.
- Seed 643: opening language prior: absent by probe digit; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A/T5. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none clean, because natural A/T5 repeats mono-ID A/T5.
- Seed 647: opening language prior: absent by probe digit; all cells open digit 2, though mono-ID A/T1 has English `DISAGREE` leakage. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B/T4 after English A, clean against flat baselines.
- Seed 653: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A/T5. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B/T4 and B/T6 after English A, new by speaker.

Overall split: iter 67 has one clear opening-prior/intensity split in mono-EN seed 601, no movement toward agreement, and several candidate cross-lingual excess hardenings. The cleaner cross candidates are inverted seed 647 B/T4, inverted seed 653 B/T4/T6, natural seed 617 B/T6, and natural seed 641 A/T3. These are amplification within the shared rights-first position, not interaction drift across the value boundary.
