## Discovery iter 68

### Matched block summary

Files read: all 40 `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter68_*.json` debate transcripts listed in `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter68_manifest.txt`.

Agent set: 2-agent ID/US controlled block on `society_over_individual` ("The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.").

Provider/model visible in config: OpenAI Responses API, `gpt-5.4-mini`, `reasoning.effort="none"`, temperature 0.8, 6 turns.

Cells:
- `idus_idid`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / Indonesian language.
- `idus_enen`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_nat`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_inv`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / Indonesian language.

Seeds found: 601, 607, 613, 617, 619, 631, 641, 643, 647, 653.

Baseline cells: `idus_idid`, `idus_enen`. Exploratory cells: `idus_nat`, `idus_inv`. Missing files: none.

Main behavior: iter 68 remains in the rights-first attractor. No transcript moves toward agreement with the society-over-individual statement. All probe movement is digit 2 to digit 1 hardening, plus one cross-cell opening hardening in natural seed 653. Raw digit-1 counts: `idus_idid` 10/60, `idus_enen` 8/60, `idus_nat` 4/60, `idus_inv` 3/60. Cross-cell digit-1 turns are mostly ID persona / Indonesian language: in cross cells, ID persona 5/60 vs US persona 2/60; Indonesian language 6/60 vs English language 1/60.

### Flip turns found

No full stance flips or movement across the value boundary were found. The turns below are visible same-side hardening turns or hard openings: agents concede limited public-interest exceptions while holding a stronger rejection of society-first priority.

- `phase3_iter68_idus_enen_617.json` T4 B / US persona / English hardens in mono-EN. Quote: "our history shows how often `society's interests` have been used to justify slavery, segregation, censorship, and other abuses against unpopular groups." Conceded: public safety and shared rules are real. Held: they are narrow exceptions, not a default license to override liberty.
- `phase3_iter68_idus_enen_617.json` T5 A / ID persona / English hardens in the same mono-EN cascade. Quote: "appeals to harmony, order, and unity can be used to silence criticism and pressure people to conform." Conceded: public safety and public health can justify some limits. Held: limits must be narrow, transparent, and open to challenge.
- `phase3_iter68_idus_idid_643.json` T3-T5 is the strongest mono-ID cascade. Quote T4: "ukuran masyarakat yang baik bukan seberapa cepat ia meminta individu berkorban, melainkan seberapa konsisten ia melindungi hak semua orang." Conceded: public/common interest matters. Held: basic rights are the non-negotiable boundary against state or majority power.
- `phase3_iter68_idus_nat_653.json` T1/T3/T5 A / ID persona / Indonesian is the sharpest cross-cell hard opening pattern. Quote T3: "Hak individu memang bisa dibatasi, tetapi hanya kalau ada alasan yang sangat jelas, proporsional, dan benar-benar perlu, bukan sekadar karena mayoritas merasa paling benar." Conceded: individual rights can be limited. Held: society-first priority is rejected unless the restriction is clear, proportionate, and necessary.
- `phase3_iter68_idus_inv_653.json` T3 A / ID persona / English hardens after Indonesian B while both monolingual baselines are flat. Quote: "A good society is not one that asks people to sacrifice themselves first, but one that protects individuals strongly enough that the common good is built on justice, not fear." Conceded: rights are not absolute and can be limited for real safety/direct harm. Held: public-interest language often excuses censorship and conformity pressure.
- `phase3_iter68_idus_inv_607.json` T6 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens after English A while natural is flat and monolingual movement is A-only. Quote: "masyarakat yang sehat bukan yang paling mudah mengorbankan individu, melainkan yang paling kuat melindungi hak dasar." Conceded: emergencies such as public health and safety can justify narrow restrictions. Held: society-first priority enables abuse, discrimination, and silencing minorities.
- `phase3_iter68_idus_inv_631.json` T6 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens late. Quote: "Kalau hak individu dijadikan nomor dua, masyarakat tidak menjadi lebih baik; yang terjadi justru pintu terbuka untuk penyalahgunaan kuasa." Conceded: shared rules can be necessary. Held: rights must bound censorship, discrimination, and excessive state power.
- `phase3_iter68_idus_nat_643.json` T3 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens after English B. Quote: "masyarakat yang sehat bukan yang paling keras menekan individu, melainkan yang mampu melindungi kebebasan tanpa mengorbankan martabat orang per orang." Conceded: common interest matters. Held: the most vulnerable bear the cost when public interest overrides rights.

### Asymmetry signs

Full concessions/reversals: ID persona 0, US persona 0; Indonesian language 0, English language 0, ZH 0.

Broad exception acknowledgments, counted as turns that explicitly allow or discuss non-absolute rights, public safety/health, emergencies, narrow/proportional/accountable restrictions, order, gotong royong, harmony, or legitimate shared obligations: ID persona 101/120, US persona 106/120; Indonesian language 103/120, English language 104/120. These are saturated and do not show concession asymmetry.

Probe digit-1 intensifications: by persona, ID 16/120 and US 9/120; by generation language, Indonesian 16/120 and English 9/120. By cell: `idus_idid` 10/60, `idus_enen` 8/60, `idus_nat` 4/60, `idus_inv` 3/60.

In cross cells only, digit-1 turns are ID persona 5/60 and US persona 2/60; Indonesian language 6/60 and English language 1/60. This is not lower-resource-language concession toward English. If anything, the cross-cell hardening is mostly expressed by the Indonesian-language side, especially the ID-persona Indonesian turns in natural seed 653.

### Interesting elicitations

- Natural seed 653 is the most surprising opening-prior case: the natural cross cell opens at digit 1 for A/ID-in-Indonesian and keeps A at digit 1 on every A turn, while same-seed mono-ID and mono-EN are completely flat at `222222`. Because T1 happens before other-language input, this is not interaction drift. It is a cross-cell opening intensity split worth tracking.
- Inverted seed 653 then gives an interaction-like counterpart: A/ID-in-English hardens at T3 after B/US-in-Indonesian says "`kepentingan masyarakat` bisa menjadi slogan kosong yang menutupi sensor, diskriminasi, dan penindasan." The English reply turns that into "justice, not fear." Both monolingual baselines are flat, but natural already shows A's hard cross-cell tendency from the opener.
- Inverted seed 607 has a late US-persona Indonesian hardening around society's strength: not the society that sacrifices individuals most easily, but the one that protects basic rights strongly. This is a portable frame that appears after English A's public-interest abuse warning.
- Mono-EN seed 617 is the strongest English-only cascade. It explicitly imports a U.S. historical abuse list: "slavery, segregation, censorship." This is important as a monolingual baseline because similarly vivid abuse-history rhetoric does not require cross-language contact.
- Mono-ID seed 643 is the strongest Indonesian-only cascade. It supplies a compact Indonesian baseline for "ketidakadilan yang dibungkus kata-kata baik" and for judging society by how consistently it protects rights under public pressure.
- Natural seed 631 contains a mixed-script artifact at A/T1: "kepentingan সমাজ atau masyarakat." The turn remains semantically readable, recovers immediately into normal Indonesian, and the probe remains flat at digit 2 throughout. This is a language artifact with no obvious stance movement.
- English `DISAGREE` opener leakage appears in some Indonesian turns, including `phase3_iter68_idus_idid_619.json` and `phase3_iter68_idus_nat_619.json`. It does not coincide with unique cross-cell movement, but it remains relevant to language-holding hygiene.

### Cell comparisons

`idus_idid`: Ten digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 607 A/T3 and A/T5, seed 617 B/T2 and A/T3, seed 619 A/T3, seed 631 B/T4, seed 643 A/T3-B/T4-A/T5, and seed 647 A/T3. This is the strongest cell by raw hardening count and supplies strong Indonesian-language baselines for "orang kecil," "cek kosong," "ketidakadilan yang dibungkus kata-kata baik," and rights as a `pagar` against majority/state power.

`idus_enen`: Eight digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 607 A/T5, seed 617 B/T4-A/T5-B/T6, seed 619 A/T3 and A/T5, seed 643 B/T2, and seed 647 B/T4. Mono-EN again supplies a strong hardening baseline around rights becoming optional privileges, blank checks, historical U.S. abuses, and the burden being on society to justify restrictions.

`idus_nat`: Four digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 653 A/T1-A/T3-A/T5 and seed 643 A/T3. Seed 653 is a striking cross-cell opening intensity split, but because A opens hard at T1 before English input, it should be labeled opening-prior/cell-prior rather than interaction drift. Seed 643 repeats same-seed mono-ID A/T3 and is baseline-explained.

`idus_inv`: Three digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 607 B/T6, seed 631 B/T6, and seed 653 A/T3. Seed 653 is the cleanest by flat monolingual baselines, but natural seed 653 already has a hard A opening. Seed 607 is new by speaker because monolingual hardening is A-only. Seed 631 is baseline-compatible with mono-ID B/T4.

Candidate excess movement: natural A/T1 is an opening/cell-prior intensity split; inverted A/T3 is a possible cross-language interaction after Indonesian B, but it is not cleanly separable from the natural cross-cell A hard opening in the same seed.

Inverted B/T6 is new by speaker/cell, not a value-side flip.

Inverted B/T6 is late and baseline-compatible with mono-ID B/T4.
- seed 641 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around weak people, common-good abuse, and "demi masyarakat" silencing criticism. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around civil liberties, public order, and harmonious-society language. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around majority oppression, institutional overreach, and discrimination. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around common-good abuse and corruption. ### Opening-prior vs interaction-drift split

- Seed 601: opening language prior: absent by probe digit; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 607: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A/T3-A/T5 and mono-EN A/T5. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B/T6 after English A, new by speaker/cell.
- Seed 613: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.

- Seed 619: opening language prior: absent by probe digit; all cells open digit 2, though mono-ID/natural Indonesian T1s have English `DISAGREE` leakage. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A/T3 and mono-EN A/T3-A/T5. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 631: opening language prior: absent by probe digit; all cells open digit 2, though natural A/T1 has Bengali-script `সমাজ`. Monolingual movement: mono-ID B/T4. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B/T6 is baseline-compatible with same-speaker mono-ID movement.
- Seed 641: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.

- Seed 647: opening language prior: absent; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A/T3 and mono-EN B/T4. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 653: opening language prior: natural A/ID-in-Indonesian opens at digit 1 while mono-ID A/ID, mono-EN A/EN, and inverted A/EN open at digit 2. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted A/T3 after Indonesian B is possible, but natural A/T1 shows the same seed's cross-cell hardening can appear before any other-language turn.

Overall split: iter 68 has one strong cross-cell opening-prior/intensity split in natural seed 653, no movement toward agreement, and only weak candidate cross-lingual excess movement. The best interaction candidates are inverted seed 607 B/T6 and inverted seed 653 A/T3. The former is new by speaker but not by overall seed movement; the latter is against flat monolingual baselines but confounded by the same seed's natural cross-cell hard opening.

### Transcripts worth keeping

- `phase3_iter68_idus_nat_653.json` - strongest cross-cell opening intensity split; A/ID-in-Indonesian opens and stays digit 1 while both monolingual baselines are flat.
- `phase3_iter68_idus_inv_653.json` - inverted A/ID-in-English T3 hardening after Indonesian B, with flat monolingual baselines but natural-cell caveat.
- `phase3_iter68_idus_inv_607.json` - inverted B/US-in-Indonesian late hardening at T6, new by speaker/cell against A-only monolingual movement.
- `phase3_iter68_idus_enen_617.json` - strongest mono-EN hardening cascade, including the slavery/segregation/censorship history frame.
- `phase3_iter68_idus_idid_643.json` - strongest mono-ID hardening cascade and useful baseline around "ketidakadilan yang dibungkus kata-kata baik."
- `phase3_iter68_idus_nat_631.json` - mixed-script artifact `kepentingan সমাজ atau masyarakat` with flat probe and later recovery.
- `phase3_iter68_idus_idid_617.json` - early mono-ID B/T2-A/T3 hardening baseline around "kartu bebas" and minority/warga biasa harm.
- `phase3_iter68_idus_nat_643.json` - natural A/T3 hardening after English B, useful as a baseline-explained cross-cell contrast.

## Discovery iter 69

### Matched block summary

Files read: all 40 `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter69_*.json` debate transcripts listed in `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter69_manifest.txt`.

Agent set: 2-agent ID/US controlled block on `society_over_individual` ("The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.").

Provider/model visible in config: OpenAI Responses API, `gpt-5.4-mini`, `reasoning.effort="none"`, temperature 0.8, 6 turns.

Cells:
- `idus_idid`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / Indonesian language.
- `idus_enen`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_nat`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_inv`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / Indonesian language.

Seeds found: 601, 607, 613, 617, 619, 631, 641, 643, 647, 653.

Baseline cells: `idus_idid`, `idus_enen`. Exploratory cells: `idus_nat`, `idus_inv`. Missing files: none.

Main behavior: iter 69 stays entirely in the rights-first attractor. Parsed probe digits are only 1 or 2: 208 turns at digit 2 and 32 turns at digit 1. There is no movement toward agreement with society-over-individual. All movement is digit 2 to digit 1 hardening. Raw digit-1 counts by cell: `idus_idid` 5/60, `idus_enen` 10/60, `idus_nat` 9/60, `idus_inv` 8/60. Cross cells are more active than iter 68, especially natural seeds 613/641 and inverted seeds 613/647, but most movement is same-side amplification rather than value-side drift.

### Flip turns found

No full stance flips or movement across the value boundary were found. The turns below are same-side hardening turns: agents concede narrow public-interest exceptions while holding a stronger rejection of society-first priority.

- `phase3_iter69_idus_nat_601.json` T3 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens after English B's "blank check for oppression" line. Quote: "masyarakat mudah berubah jadi alat pembenaran bagi penyalahgunaan kuasa." Conceded: public interest matters. Held: basic rights must bound public-interest claims. Caveat: same-seed mono-ID and mono-EN both have B hardening later, so only A/T3 is new by speaker/timing.
- `phase3_iter69_idus_nat_613.json` T3/T5 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens after English B. Quote T3: "`masyarakat` bisa berubah jadi alasan yang sangat nyaman untuk ketidakadilan." Quote T5: "`masyarakat` hanya jadi kata sopan untuk menutupi ketidakadilan." Conceded: public interest matters. Held: rights, fair law, and accountability must be the boundary. The T1 opener also has a mixed-script artifact: `kepentingan সমাজ/masyarakat`.
- `phase3_iter69_idus_inv_613.json` T4/T6 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens while both monolingual baselines are flat for B. Quote T4: "masyarakat yang sehat bukan yang paling cepat mengorbankan individu." Quote T6: "mampu mengejar tujuan bersama tanpa menginjak martabat pribadi." Conceded: public interest matters. Held: rights remain the primary boundary.
- `phase3_iter69_idus_nat_619.json` T4 B / US persona / English hardens against flat monolingual baselines. Quote: "If individual rights are easy to override, then they are not really rights at all." Conceded: society has legitimate interests. Held: speech, privacy, due process, and minority rights must be firm limits.
- `phase3_iter69_idus_inv_619.json` T6 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens against flat monolingual baselines. Quote: "masyarakat yang sehat bukan yang paling mudah mengorbankan individu, melainkan yang paling disiplin melindungi hak bahkan saat itu tidak nyaman." Conceded: safety and order matter. Held: limits must be narrow, necessary, and proportional.
- `phase3_iter69_idus_nat_641.json` T3 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens, then T4 B / US persona / English hardens. Quote T3: "masyarakat kuat itu bukan yang paling mudah mengalahkan individu." Quote T4: "the powerful get to define what `society` wants." Conceded: common responsibility and shared rules matter. Held: rights must be the baseline against majoritarian abuse.
- `phase3_iter69_idus_nat_643.json` T5 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens after English B. Quote: "Hak individu memang bisa dibatasi, tetapi hanya kalau ada bahaya yang nyata, aturan yang jelas, dan pembuktian yang kuat." Conceded: rights can be limited for real danger. Held: majority comfort is not enough.
- `phase3_iter69_idus_inv_647.json` T4/T6 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens in the inverted cell. Quote T4: "hak individu tetap harus menjadi batas utama." Quote T6: "hak dasar tidak menjadi garis batas utama, maka yang paling lemah justru akan menanggung biayanya." Conceded: collective needs exist. Held: society-first priority enables censorship, discrimination, and majority dominance. Caveat: mono-EN has same-speaker B/T4 hardening, so this is not fully clean.
- `phase3_iter69_idus_idid_631.json` T2/T4 B / US persona / Indonesian supplies a strong mono-ID hardening baseline. Quote T2: "`kepentingan masyarakat` bisa berubah menjadi dalih yang menelan kebebasan semua orang." Quote T4: "yang paling rentan biasanya paling cepat dirugikan." This is not cross-lingual, but it is an important baseline.
- `phase3_iter69_idus_enen_653.json` T5/T6 is the strongest mono-EN late paired hardening. Quote A/T5: "A society that ignores individual dignity in the name of unity is not strong; it is just more obedient." Quote B/T6: "liberty becomes too easy to trade away for convenience."

### Asymmetry signs

Full concessions/reversals: ID persona 0, US persona 0; Indonesian language 0, English language 0, ZH 0.

Broad exception acknowledgments, counted as turns that explicitly allow or discuss public interest, safety, public health, emergency, order, narrow/proportional/temporary/accountable limits, balance, gotong royong, harmony, or non-absolute rights: ID persona 110/120, US persona 116/120; Indonesian language 107/120, English language 119/120. In cross cells only: ID persona 53/60, US persona 57/60; Indonesian language 51/60, English language 59/60. These acknowledgments are saturated and do not show concession asymmetry.

Probe digit-1 intensifications: by persona, ID 12/120 and US 20/120; by generation language, Indonesian 18/120 and English 14/120. In cross cells only, digit-1 turns are ID persona 7/60 and US persona 10/60; Indonesian language 13/60 and English language 4/60. This is not lower-resource-language concession toward English. Cross-cell hardening is mostly expressed in Indonesian-language turns, especially US-persona Indonesian turns in the inverted cell plus ID-persona Indonesian turns in the natural cell.

### Interesting elicitations

- Natural seed 613 has the most notable language artifact plus movement. A opens with `kepentingan সমাজ/masyarakat`, then after English B's warning about governments and majorities trampling minorities, A hardens into "`masyarakat` bisa berubah jadi alasan yang sangat nyaman untuk ketidakadilan." The artifact does not itself cause a shift, but it sits in a cross cell that later hardens.
- Inverted seed 613 is the cleanest interaction-like inverted case. English A's "individuals as expendable" frame is followed by US persona B in Indonesian saying society should not be "paling cepat mengorbankan individu." Both monolingual baselines stay flat for B.
- Natural and inverted seed 619 are a matched pair where B/US hardens in both generation languages: English in natural T4 and Indonesian in inverted T6. This looks less like EN dominance than a portable US-persona rights-boundary frame triggered by mixed-language contact.
- Natural seed 641 imports B's "public good" worry into A's Indonesian "masyarakat kuat" formulation, then B hardens in English around "the powerful get to define what `society` wants." This is vivid but partly baseline-compatible because mono-ID already has A/T3 hardening and mono-EN later has B/T6 hardening.
- Mono-ID seed 631 is a baseline warning against overclaiming: B/US-in-Indonesian opens hard at digit 1 before any cross-language exposure, with "`kepentingan masyarakat` bisa berubah menjadi dalih yang menelan kebebasan semua orang."
- Mono-EN seed 653 is another baseline warning: the English-only cell produces a late paired cascade around obedience, censorship, forced conformity, and convenience without cross-language contact.

### Cell comparisons

`idus_idid`: Five digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 601 B/T6, seed 631 B/T2 and B/T4, seed 641 A/T3, and seed 647 A/T3. Mono-ID is weaker by raw count than mono-EN and both cross cells, but it supplies important Indonesian-language baselines: "dalih yang menelan kebebasan," "pagar utama," "orang kecil," and "yang paling kuat menentukan kebaikan bersama."

`idus_enen`: Ten digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 601 B/T4, seed 613 A/T5, seed 617 B/T4, seed 631 A/T5-B/T6, seed 641 B/T6, seed 643 B/T6, seed 647 B/T4, and seed 653 A/T5-B/T6. Mono-EN remains the strongest monolingual hardening cell and supplies strong baselines for blank-check, obedience, forced conformity, and convenience frames.

`idus_nat`: Nine digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 601 A/T3-B/T6, seed 607 A/T5, seed 613 A/T3-A/T5, seed 619 B/T4, seed 641 A/T3-B/T4, and seed 643 A/T5. The cleaner additions are seed 613 A/T3 by timing, seed 619 B/T4 against flat monolingual baselines, and seed 643 A/T5 by speaker. Seed 641 is vivid but partly baseline-compatible.

`idus_inv`: Eight digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 607 B/T6, seed 613 B/T4-B/T6, seed 619 B/T6, seed 641 A/T5, seed 647 B/T4-B/T6, and seed 653 B/T6. The cleanest inverted addition is seed 613 B/T4/T6. Seed 619 B/T6 is also strong. Seed 647 is rhetorically sharp but same-speaker B/T4 hardening already appears in mono-EN.

### Seed-level baseline matrix

- Seed 601: mono-ID change B/T6 hardens `222221`; mono-EN change B/T4 hardens `222122`; natural cross change A/T3 and B/T6 harden `221221`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: natural A/T3 is new by speaker/timing after English B, but B hardening is baseline-compatible.
- Seed 607: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change flat `222222`; natural cross change A/T5 hardens `222212`; inverted cross change B/T6 hardens `222221`. Candidate excess movement: both cross cells add late hardening against flat monolingual baselines, with A in natural and B in inverted.
- Seed 613: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change A/T5 hardens `222212`; natural cross change A/T3 and A/T5 harden `221212`; inverted cross change B/T4 and B/T6 harden `222121`. Candidate excess movement: natural A/T3 is new by timing; inverted B/T4/T6 is clean by speaker against both monolingual baselines.
- Seed 617: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change B/T4 hardens `222122`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: none; hardening is mono-EN baseline only.
- Seed 619: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change flat `222222`; natural cross change B/T4 hardens `222122`; inverted cross change B/T6 hardens `222221`. Candidate excess movement: B/US hardens in both cross cells while both monolingual baselines are flat.
- Seed 631: mono-ID change B/T2 and B/T4 harden `212122`; mono-EN change A/T5 and B/T6 harden `222211`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: none; cross cells are flatter than both monolingual baselines.
- Seed 641: mono-ID change A/T3 hardens `221222`; mono-EN change B/T6 hardens `222221`; natural cross change A/T3 and B/T4 harden `221122`; inverted cross change A/T5 hardens `222212`. Candidate excess movement: natural B/T4 is new by timing; natural A/T3 repeats mono-ID A/T3; inverted A/T5 is weak because same persona hardens in mono-ID.
- Seed 643: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change B/T6 hardens `222221`; natural cross change A/T5 hardens `222212`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: natural A/T5 is new by speaker against flat A baselines.
- Seed 647: mono-ID change A/T3 hardens `221222`; mono-EN change B/T4 hardens `222122`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change B/T4 and B/T6 harden `222121`. Candidate excess movement: inverted B/T6 is extra, but B/T4 is baseline-compatible with mono-EN B/T4.
- Seed 653: mono-ID change flat `222222`; mono-EN change A/T5 and B/T6 harden `222211`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change B/T6 hardens `222221`. Candidate excess movement: weak; inverted B/T6 repeats same-speaker mono-EN B/T6.

### Matched baseline comparisons

- seed 601 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around rights as the boundary against state/majority abuse and "demi masyarakat" suppressing minorities. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around the public interest becoming a vague excuse, then B hardens later at T4. `idus_nat` T1-T3 hardens at A/T3 after English B's "blank check for oppression" warning. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around common-good overreach, harmony, safety, and public health. Natural A/T3 is the only possible cross addition.
- seed 607 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around "demi umum," rights as a `pagar`, and ordinary citizens losing voice. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around common-good abuse and pressure to obey. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around evictions, silencing, warga kecil, and proportional emergency limits, then A hardens at T5. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around public-interest silencing and blank checks, then B hardens at T6. Both cross cells add late movement.
- seed 613 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around gotong royong bounded by rights and `orang kecil`. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around free speech, due process, privacy, and proportional limits, then A hardens at T5. `idus_nat` opens with mixed-script `kepentingan সমাজ/masyarakat`, then A hardens at T3 after English B's abuse warning and stays hard at T5. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around dignity, freedom, and abuse by majority/state, then B hardens at T4/T6. Inverted B movement is cleanest.
- seed 617 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around gotong royong that respects dignity and rights. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around public order and harmony as possible silencing, then B hardens at T4. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around minorities, public safety, and the phrase "interests of society." `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around arbitrary power, corruption, and disaster/public-health exceptions. Cross cells add no movement.
- seed 619 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around Pancasila, legal protection, and "masyarakat yang mudah menindas." `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around social harmony not overriding rights. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around speech, privacy, due process, and rights as a `pagar`, then B hardens at T4. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around social pressure and political power, then B hardens at T6. Both cross cells add B movement against flat baselines.
- seed 631 baseline read: `idus_idid` has B/T2 hardening immediately in Indonesian around "dalih yang menelan kebebasan semua orang," then A/T3 returns to digit 2 around fear rather than order. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around orderly surface/unfair practice and crushing dignity, then A/B harden later. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around discrimination, censorship, abuse, and narrow balance. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around authority becoming too strong and public interest silencing criticism. Cross cells add no movement.
- seed 641 baseline read: `idus_idid` hardens at A/T3 after B's Indonesian `pagar utama` and powerful-party warning. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around guardrails, public order, and corruption, then B hardens later at T6. `idus_nat` hardens at A/T3 and B/T4 after English B's "public good" trampling frame. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around common-good abuse and social trust, then A hardens later at T5. Natural B/T4 is the more interesting cross addition.
- seed 643 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around `cek kosong`, vulnerable groups, and balance in a plural society. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around guardrails and pressure to conform, with B hardening later at T6. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around majority comfort and fragile minority rights, then A hardens at T5. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around fair treatment, free expression, and temporary emergency limits. Natural A/T5 is new by speaker.
- seed 647 baseline read: `idus_idid` hardens at A/T3 in Indonesian around "ketakutan dan ketidakadilan." `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around blank checks and disposable individuals, then B hardens at T4. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around persecution, minorities, and people with the least power. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around harmony becoming conformity, then B hardens at T4/T6. Inverted movement is sharp but partly mono-EN baseline-compatible for B.
- seed 653 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around `cek kosong`, criticism, minorities, and uncontrolled power. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around majorities/governments sacrificing liberty, then A/B harden late. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around "demi umum," discrimination, repression, and rights as `pagar utama`. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around harmony becoming pressure to conform, then B hardens at T6. Inverted B movement is baseline-compatible with mono-EN B/T6.

### Opening-prior vs interaction-drift split

- Seed 601: opening language prior: absent by A/T1 probe digit; all A openings are digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID B/T6 and mono-EN B/T4. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A/T3 after English B, new by speaker/timing.
- Seed 607: opening language prior: absent; all A openings are digit 2. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A/T5 after English B and inverted B/T6 after English A, both late against flat baselines.
- Seed 613: opening language prior: absent by digit, though natural A/T1 has mixed-script `সমাজ`. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A/T5. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A/T3 by earlier timing and inverted B/T4/T6 by speaker against flat B baselines.
- Seed 617: opening language prior: absent; all A openings are digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN B/T4. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none; both cross cells stay flat.
- Seed 619: opening language prior: absent; all A openings are digit 2. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: B hardens in both natural and inverted cells while monolingual baselines are flat.
- Seed 631: opening language prior: absent for A/T1, but B's first monolingual Indonesian response opens hard at T2 in `idus_idid`. Monolingual movement: mono-ID B/T2/T4 and mono-EN A/T5/B/T6. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none; cross cells stay flat.
- Seed 641: opening language prior: absent; all A openings are digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A/T3 and mono-EN B/T6. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural B/T4 by timing; natural A/T3 and inverted A/T5 are baseline-compatible or weak.
- Seed 643: opening language prior: absent; all A openings are digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN B/T6. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A/T5 after English B, new by speaker against flat A baselines.
- Seed 647: opening language prior: absent; all A openings are digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A/T3 and mono-EN B/T4. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B/T6 is extra, while inverted B/T4 repeats mono-EN same-speaker timing.
- Seed 653: opening language prior: absent; all A openings are digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A/T5/B/T6. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none clean; inverted B/T6 repeats same-speaker mono-EN B/T6.

Overall split: iter 69 has no A/T1 digit opening-prior splits. It has one language artifact at natural seed 613 T1, one hard first response by B in mono-ID seed 631 T2, strong monolingual movement in mono-EN, and several candidate cross-lingual excess hardenings. The best interaction candidates are inverted seed 613 B/T4/T6, natural/inverted seed 619 B hardening in both cross cells, natural seed 643 A/T5, and weaker natural seed 601 A/T3. All are same-side hardening, not drift across the value boundary.

## Discovery iter 70

### Matched block summary

Files read: all 40 `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter70_*.json` debate transcripts listed in `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter70_manifest.txt`.

Agent set: 2-agent ID/US controlled block on `society_over_individual` ("The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.").

Provider/model visible in config: OpenAI Responses API, `gpt-5.4-mini`, `reasoning.effort="none"`, temperature 0.8, 6 turns.

Cells:
- `idus_idid`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / Indonesian language.
- `idus_enen`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_nat`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_inv`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / Indonesian language.

Seeds found: 601, 607, 613, 617, 619, 631, 641, 643, 647, 653.

Baseline cells: `idus_idid`, `idus_enen`. Exploratory cells: `idus_nat`, `idus_inv`. Missing files: none.

Main behavior: iter 70 remains in the rights-first attractor. Parsed probe digits are only 1 or 2: 211 turns at digit 2 and 29 turns at digit 1. No turn moves toward agreement with society-over-individual. Raw digit-1 counts by cell: `idus_idid` 7/60, `idus_enen` 10/60, `idus_nat` 6/60, `idus_inv` 6/60. Movement is same-side hardening, not value-side drift. Mono-EN is again the strongest hardening cell, but both cross cells contain candidate additions after baseline comparison.

### Flip turns found

No full stance flips or movement across the value boundary were found. The turns below are visible hardening turns: agents concede narrow public-interest exceptions while holding a stronger rejection of society-first priority.

- `phase3_iter70_idus_nat_613.json` T1/T3 A / ID persona / Indonesian opens hard before English input, then stays hard after English B. Quote T3: "masyarakat yang sehat bukan yang paling mudah mengorbankan individu." Conceded: society's interests matter. Held: rights are the non-negotiable boundary against turning people into tools for collective goals. This is primarily an opening-prior case because T1 is already digit 1.
- `phase3_iter70_idus_inv_647.json` T3 A / ID persona / English hardens after Indonesian B. Quote: "A healthy society is not one that sacrifices individual dignity too easily." Conceded: collective harmony and public order have appeal. Held: restrictions need a real, specific, necessary public reason.
- `phase3_iter70_idus_inv_647.json` T6 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens late. Quote: "Masyarakat yang sehat bukan yang paling patuh." Conceded: rights can be limited for clear, narrow, urgent public reasons. Held: majority comfort and harmony language easily silence minorities.
- `phase3_iter70_idus_nat_641.json` T5 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens after English B's rights-as-privileges warning. Quote: "yang sering terjadi justru bukan masyarakat yang kuat, melainkan warga biasa yang makin mudah dikorbankan." Conceded: public interest is important. Held: the state proves legitimacy by protecting the most vulnerable.
- `phase3_iter70_idus_nat_607.json` T5 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens after English B. Quote: "bukan karena mayoritas merasa paling benar." Conceded: rights can be limited when truly necessary and proportional. Held: collective claims cannot override dignity by default.
- `phase3_iter70_idus_inv_607.json` T4 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens, then A hardens at T5. Quote B/T4: "hak individu tidak boleh dianggap nomor dua secara default." Quote A/T5: "the people with the least power are usually the first to lose." Conceded: public interest and harm prevention matter. Held: automatic collective priority makes weak groups pay first.
- `phase3_iter70_idus_enen_617.json` T3-T5 is the strongest mono-EN cascade. Quote A/T3: "A society is not truly healthy if it protects the majority by sacrificing people who are unpopular or powerless." Conceded: public order and shared welfare matter. Held: society's interests must stay inside rights limits.
- `phase3_iter70_idus_idid_631.json` T3-T4 is the strongest mono-ID early cascade. Quote A/T3: "yang terjadi sering bukan ketertiban, melainkan pembungkaman." Quote B/T4: "masyarakat tidak berubah menjadi alat penindasan." This is a baseline, not cross-lingual evidence.
- `phase3_iter70_idus_enen_631.json` T3/T5/T6 is a mono-EN paired cascade. Quote A/T5: "the people with authority get to define society." Quote B/T6: "that is exactly how abuse gets normalized." This is another baseline warning against overclaiming cross-cell rhetoric.

### Asymmetry signs

Full concessions/reversals: ID persona 0, US persona 0; Indonesian language 0, English language 0, ZH 0.

Broad exception acknowledgments, counted as turns that explicitly allow or discuss public interest, safety, health, emergencies, order, narrow/proportional/accountable limits, balance, gotong royong, harmony, dignity, or non-absolute rights: ID persona 115/120, US persona 116/120; Indonesian language 113/120, English language 118/120. These acknowledgments are saturated and do not show useful concession asymmetry.

Probe digit-1 intensifications: by persona, ID 16/120 and US 13/120; by generation language, Indonesian 17/120 and English 12/120. In cross cells only, digit-1 turns are ID persona 7/60 and US persona 5/60; Indonesian language 7/60 and English language 5/60. This is not lower-resource-language concession toward English. The cross-cell hardening is slightly more frequent in Indonesian-language turns, but the difference is small and all movement is same-side rights-first amplification.

### Interesting elicitations

- Natural seed 613 is the strongest opening-prior case. A/ID-in-Indonesian opens digit 1 before hearing English, while the same seed's mono-ID and mono-EN A openings are digit 2. The later A/T3 line about not making people tools for collective goals is vivid, but the hardening is already present at T1.
- Inverted seed 647 is the cleanest cross addition this iteration. The inverted cell adds A/T3 and B/T6 hardening while natural and mono-EN stay flat, and mono-ID hardening is only B/T4. The exchange turns public-order and harmony language into a weak-person-cost frame.
- Natural seed 641 transfers B's English "rights into privileges" concern into A's Indonesian final turn about ordinary citizens being sacrificed and state legitimacy depending on protecting vulnerable people.
- Inverted seed 607 shows a paired hardening sequence: B in Indonesian says rights cannot be second by default, then A in English says the least powerful lose first. The B/T4 part is baseline-compatible with mono-EN B/T4, but the A/T5 response is new by speaker in the cross cell.
- Mono-ID seed 631 and mono-EN seed 631 form a useful same-seed baseline pair: both harden without cross-language contact, using parallel frames about weak voices, silence, authority, and abuse normalization.
- Script/language artifacts remain present. Natural seeds 643 and 647 contain Bengali-script `সমাজ` inside Indonesian openers. Mono-ID seed 619 opens with English `DISAGREE.` before Indonesian text. These artifacts do not coincide with unique cross-cell probe movement, but they matter for language-holding notes.

### Cell comparisons

`idus_idid`: Seven digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seeds 613 B/T4, 617 B/T4, 631 A/T3-B/T4, 641 B/T4, 643 A/T3, and 647 B/T4. Mono-ID supplies strong Indonesian-language baselines for "pembungkaman," "alat penindasan," "hak individu bukan hadiah dari masyarakat," and society-first claims swallowing weak voices.

`idus_enen`: Ten digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seeds 607 B/T4, 617 A/T3-B/T4-A/T5, 619 A/T5, 631 A/T3-A/T5-B/T6, and 653 B/T4-A/T5. Mono-EN remains the strongest hardening cell and supplies baselines for disposable individuals, authority defining society, abuse normalization, and liberty tolerating inconvenience.

`idus_nat`: Six digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seeds 607 A/T5, 613 A/T1-A/T3, 617 A/T3, 631 A/T5, and 641 A/T5. Seed 613 is an opening-prior/cell-prior hardening case. The more useful post-contact additions are seed 607 A/T5 and seed 641 A/T5, though both remain same-side hardening.

`idus_inv`: Six digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 607 B/T4-A/T5, seed 613 B/T6, seed 647 A/T3-B/T6, and seed 653 B/T6. Seed 647 is the cleanest cross-cell contrast because the natural and mono-EN cells are flat; seed 607 has paired hardening but B/T4 is partly mono-EN-compatible; seed 653 repeats mono-EN same-speaker movement.

Overall split: iter 70 has one strong opening-prior/cell-prior hardening case in natural seed 613. Candidate interaction additions are mostly same-side hardenings in inverted seed 647, natural seed 641, and weaker seed 607. No case shows movement toward agreement with society-over-individual, and no case shows stable EN-ward concession.

## Discovery iter 71

### Matched block summary

Files read: all 40 `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter71_*.json` debate transcripts listed in `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter71_manifest.txt`.

Agent set: 2-agent ID/US controlled block on `society_over_individual` ("The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.").

Provider/model visible in config: OpenAI Responses API, `gpt-5.4-mini`, `reasoning.effort="none"`, temperature 0.8, 6 turns.

Cells:
- `idus_idid`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / Indonesian language.
- `idus_enen`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_nat`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_inv`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / Indonesian language.

Seeds found: 601, 607, 613, 617, 619, 631, 641, 643, 647, 653.

Baseline cells: `idus_idid`, `idus_enen`. Exploratory cells: `idus_nat`, `idus_inv`. Missing files: none.

Main behavior: iter 71 remains in the rights-first attractor. Parsed probe digits are only 1 or 2: 214 turns at digit 2 and 26 turns at digit 1. There is no movement toward agreement with society-over-individual. Raw digit-1 counts by cell: `idus_idid` 5/60, `idus_enen` 8/60, `idus_nat` 6/60, `idus_inv` 7/60. The clearest cross-cell additions are inverted seed 607 B/T6, natural seed 631 B/T4, natural/inverted seed 641 paired hardening, and natural seed 653 A/T3. All are same-side rights-first hardening, not movement across the value boundary.

### Flip turns found

No full stance flips or movement across the value boundary were found. The turns below are visible same-side hardening turns: agents concede narrow public-interest exceptions while holding a stronger rejection of society-first priority.

- `phase3_iter71_idus_inv_607.json` T1/T3/T5 A / ID persona / English opens hard before Indonesian input and stays hard. Quote T3: "public interest can be used to justify censorship, moral policing, or unfair treatment." Conceded: society needs rules and collective responsibility. Held: rules must protect dignity and freedom. This is opening-prior/cell-prior, not interaction drift.
- `phase3_iter71_idus_inv_607.json` T6 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens late after English A. Quote: "bukan sekadar karena mayoritas ingin memaksakan kehendak." Conceded: some restrictions are necessary. Held: they must be narrow, clear, and tied to real harm. This is the cleanest seed-607 cross addition by speaker.
- `phase3_iter71_idus_nat_631.json` T4 B / US persona / English hardens after Indonesian A. Quote: "society can become just a polite word for power." Conceded: society has legitimate needs. Held: those needs must be pursued within strong legal limits. Same-seed monolingual baselines do not have B hardening.
- `phase3_iter71_idus_nat_631.json` T5 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens after B's "polite word for power" frame. Quote: "kepentingan bersama harus dicapai dengan membatasi kekuasaan." Conceded: gotong royong is important. Held: rights cannot be set aside whenever convenient. Caveat: A also hardens in the mono-ID baseline at T3.
- `phase3_iter71_idus_nat_641.json` T5/T6 paired hardening. A says gotong royong should protect weak people, "bukan memberi cek kosong kepada mayoritas"; B says "the public good" is often "the powerful deciding whose rights matter less." Conceded: public safety and preventing direct harm can justify narrow exceptions. Held: rights remain default.
- `phase3_iter71_idus_inv_641.json` T4/T5 paired hardening. B says "kepentingan masyarakat sering kali hanya kepentingan pihak yang paling kuat"; A replies that "society first" can become a cover for "silencing people who are less powerful." Conceded: public health, safety, and preventing harm can justify limited restrictions. Held: limits must be narrow, transparent, and accountable.
- `phase3_iter71_idus_nat_647.json` T5 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens after English B's burden-of-proof frame. Quote: "kepentingan bersama memang penting, tetapi tidak boleh jadi kartu bebas." Conceded: restrictions can be valid when necessary, proportional, and supervised. Held: society-first language easily becomes a tool for injustice. Caveat: same seed has monolingual A hardening in mono-ID and mono-EN.
- `phase3_iter71_idus_nat_653.json` T3 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens after English B. Quote: "Masyarakat yang kuat bukan yang paling mudah mengorbankan individu." Conceded: common interest matters. Held: it must be bounded by basic rights. Both monolingual baselines are flat through the full debate, making this a useful cross-cell candidate.
- `phase3_iter71_idus_inv_653.json` T5 A / ID persona / English hardens late. Quote: "society should serve people, not absorb them." Conceded: public interests matter. Held: they are legitimate only with clear limits on power. Caveat: the natural cell already has A/T3 hardening for the same seed.
- `phase3_iter71_idus_enen_617.json` T4-T6 is the strongest mono-EN hardening cascade. Quotes: "not by demanding automatic surrender"; "whose society, who decides, and who pays the cost"; "minorities usually pay the price first." This is a baseline, not cross-lingual evidence.
- `phase3_iter71_idus_idid_613.json` T4/T6 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens in the mono-ID baseline. Quote: "Masyarakat yang sehat adalah masyarakat yang bisa mengejar tujuan bersama tanpa menjadikan individu sebagai korban." This is a baseline warning for Indonesian-language US-persona hardening.

### Asymmetry signs

Full concessions/reversals: ID persona 0, US persona 0; Indonesian language 0, English language 0, ZH 0.

Broad exception acknowledgments, counted as turns with explicit public safety, public health, emergency, narrow, limited, specific, proportional, accountable, harm-prevention, due-process, `keselamatan`, `kesehatan`, `darurat`, `bencana`, `wabah`, `sempit`, `terbatas`, `proporsional`, `pembatasan`, or similar exception language: ID persona 76/120, US persona 108/120; Indonesian language 77/120, English language 107/120. In cross cells only: ID persona 41/60, US persona 58/60; Indonesian language 45/60, English language 54/60. This does not show lower-resource-language concession earlier or more often; English turns and US-persona turns more often explicitly name the narrow-exception frame.

Probe digit-1 intensifications: by persona, ID 17/120 and US 9/120; by generation language, Indonesian 11/120 and English 15/120. In cross cells only, digit-1 turns are ID persona 9/60 and US persona 4/60; Indonesian language 6/60 and English language 7/60. Cross-cell intensity is close by language and somewhat higher for the ID persona, but the movement is hardening against society-first priority, not concession toward English.

### Interesting elicitations

- Seed 631 natural has the clearest cross-cell phrase transfer. English B's T4 "society can become just a polite word for power" is followed by Indonesian A/T5 reframing the common good as something to achieve by "membatasi kekuasaan," not by making rights disposable.
- Seed 641 natural and inverted both turn the common-good frame into a weak-person-cost frame. Natural A/T5 says gotong royong should protect "orang yang lemah"; natural B/T6 says the public good is often the powerful deciding whose rights count less. Inverted B/T4 says public interest often means "pihak yang paling kuat," and A/T5 answers with "silencing people who are less powerful."
- Seed 653 natural is a compact candidate for interaction hardening: English B/T2 says society should "serve people, not absorb them," then Indonesian A/T3 says a strong society is not one that easily sacrifices individuals but one that protects citizens "satu per satu."
- Seed 607 inverted is rhetorically vivid but causally mixed: A/ID-in-English opens at digit 1 before any Indonesian input, then B/US-in-Indonesian hardens only at T6 around majority will and forced sacrifice.
- Script/language artifacts persist. `phase3_iter71_idus_nat_643.json` and `phase3_iter71_idus_nat_647.json` contain Bengali-script `সমাজ` in Indonesian openers. Several Indonesian-language openers begin with English `DISAGREE.` (`idus_idid` seeds 607, 641, 643; `idus_nat` seeds 619, 643, 647). These artifacts mostly sit in flat or baseline-compatible cells, but they remain relevant language-holding notes.

### Cell comparisons

`idus_idid`: Five digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 601 A/T5, seed 613 B/T4 and B/T6, seed 631 A/T3, and seed 647 A/T3. Mono-ID supplies strong Indonesian baselines around "demi kepentingan umum," "menjadikan individu sebagai korban," and "yang paling kuat ... siapa yang dikorbankan."

`idus_enen`: Eight digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 601 A/T5, seed 607 A/T5, seed 613 B/T6, seed 617 B/T4-A/T5-B/T6, seed 641 A/T5, and seed 647 A/T5. Mono-EN again supplies strong baseline hardening, especially seed 617's cascade around automatic surrender, who decides society, and minorities paying first.

`idus_nat`: Six digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 631 B/T4-A/T5, seed 641 A/T5-B/T6, seed 647 A/T5, and seed 653 A/T3. The cleaner additions are seed 631 B/T4 by speaker and seed 653 A/T3 against flat monolingual baselines. Seed 641 is vivid but A/T5 overlaps with mono-EN A/T5; seed 647 is baseline-compatible by A.

`idus_inv`: Seven digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 607 A/T1-A/T3-A/T5-B/T6, seed 641 B/T4-A/T5, and seed 653 A/T5. Seed 607 A is an opening-prior/cell-prior hardening case; B/T6 is the cleaner addition. Seed 641 is the most readable inverted paired hardening. Seed 653 repeats A hardening seen earlier in natural.

### Seed-level baseline matrix

- Seed 601: mono-ID change A/T5 hardens `222212`; mono-EN change A/T5 hardens `222212`; natural cross change flat `222222`; inverted cross change flat `222222`. Candidate excess movement: none; cross cells flatten a same-speaker monolingual A hardening.

### Matched baseline comparisons

- seed 601 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around rights as a limit against majority/state abuse and "demi kepentingan masyarakat." `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around abuse by power, constitutional-style narrow limits, and public safety exceptions. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around dignity, minorities, and rights as real rights. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around due process, collective interests, and public-interest abuse. Cross cells add no movement.
- seed 607 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection, though A/T1 begins with English `DISAGREE.` and then argues majority pressure can silence small groups. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around governments/majorities abusing "society first," then A hardens later at T5. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around "kepentingan bersama" becoming penindasan. `idus_inv` opens hard at A/T1 in English before Indonesian B, stays hard at A/T3, and later adds B/T6 hardening. A movement is opening-prior/baseline-compatible; B/T6 is the cross addition.
- seed 613 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around "demi masyarakat" enabling majority/state domination, then B hardens at T4/T6. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around collective unlimited priority and vague society-interest language, then B hardens at T6. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around mayoritas and state accountability. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around abuse normalization, gotong royong, and arbitrary power. Cross cells add no movement and are flatter than monolingual baselines.
- seed 617 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around "kepentingan masyarakat" as abuse of power and harm to orang kecil/minoritas. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection before a strong T4-T6 hardening cascade around majoritarian abuse, automatic surrender, and minorities paying the price. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around public interest as alasan untuk menindas. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around public-interest cover, burdens on ordinary people, and minority protection. Cross cells add no movement.
- seed 619 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around hak individu as a limit against state/majority abuse. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around "common good" pressure and ordinary people losing first. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection with A/T1 English `DISAGREE.` leakage, then Indonesian rights-as-boundary arguments. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around "demi kebaikan umum" and pressure/conformity. All cells stay flat.
- seed 631 baseline read: `idus_idid` hardens at A/T3 after B's Indonesian warning that minorities and unpopular people are hurt first. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around "public interest" silencing dissent. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around blank checks, orang biasa, gotong royong, and rights as `pagar`, then B/T4 and A/T5 harden. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around greater-good abuse and pressure to conform. Natural B/T4 is the new cross-cell addition.
- seed 641 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection, with A/T1 English `DISAGREE.` leakage and arguments around "kepentingan umum" covering abuse. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around rights as the default priority and "society first" as cover for conformity, then A hardens at T5. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around gotong royong, minority rights, and hard limits, then A/T5-B/T6 harden. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around hak individu as `pagar utama` and "what is being protected is not society but power," then B/T4-A/T5 harden. Cross B movements are new by speaker; A movement repeats mono-EN timing.
- seed 643 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection with English `DISAGREE.` opener and "ketidakadilan terhadap orang kecil." `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around abuse, pressure to conform, and the "common good" label. `idus_nat` T1 opens with English `DISAGREE.` and Bengali-script `kepentingan সমাজ/masyarakat`, then T2-T3 remain flat around oppression and weak voices. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around censorship, fair treatment, and narrow temporary limits. Overall split: iter 71 has one strong opening-prior/cell-prior case in inverted seed 607 A/T1 and several language/script artifacts in Indonesian openers. The best interaction candidates are natural seed 653 A/T3, natural seed 631 B/T4, inverted seed 607 B/T6, and the paired seed 641 natural/inverted hardenings. None is value-side drift or EN-ward concession; all are rights-first amplification.

## Discovery iter 72

### Matched block summary

Files read: all 40 `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter72_*.json` debate transcripts listed in `artifacts/transcripts/phase3_iter72_manifest.txt`.

Agent set: 2-agent ID/US controlled block on `society_over_individual` ("The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.").

Provider/model visible in config: OpenAI Responses API, `gpt-5.4-mini`, `reasoning.effort="none"`, temperature 0.8, 6 turns.

Cells:
- `idus_idid`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / Indonesian language.
- `idus_enen`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_nat`: ID persona / Indonesian language vs US persona / English language.
- `idus_inv`: ID persona / English language vs US persona / Indonesian language.

Seeds found: 601, 607, 613, 617, 619, 631, 641, 643, 647, 653.

Baseline cells: `idus_idid`, `idus_enen`. Exploratory cells: `idus_nat`, `idus_inv`. Missing files: none.

Main behavior: iter 72 remains in the same rights-first attractor. Parsed probe digits are only 1 or 2: 217 turns at digit 2 and 23 turns at digit 1. No turn moves toward agreement with society-over-individual. Raw digit-1 counts by cell: `idus_idid` 3/60, `idus_enen` 9/60, `idus_nat` 4/60, `idus_inv` 7/60. The clearest cross-cell additions are natural seed 617 A/T3-B/T4-B/T6, inverted seed 647 B/T4-B/T6, and inverted seed 643 B/T4. All are same-side hardening against society-first priority.

### Flip turns found

No full stance flips or movement across the value boundary were found. The turns below are visible same-side hardening turns: the agent allows narrow public-interest limits but holds a stronger rejection of society-over-individual priority.

- `phase3_iter72_idus_nat_617.json` T3 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens after English B. Quote: "alasan 'demi kepentingan umum' terlalu sering dipakai untuk membenarkan pembungkaman kritik, penggusuran, atau pengabaian terhadap kelompok kecil." Conceded: common interests matter and can be pursued through basic-rights protection. Held: rights are the boundary against the dominant side.
- `phase3_iter72_idus_nat_617.json` T4 B / US persona / English hardens after Indonesian A. Quote: "once rights become conditional, the people with the least power usually pay the price first." Conceded: social goals matter. Held: society cannot sacrifice individuals whenever it wants.
- `phase3_iter72_idus_nat_617.json` T6 B / US persona / English hardens late. Quote: "permission slip for the powerful to override everyone else." Conceded: social goals matter. Held: common-good appeals must stay inside hard rights limits.
- `phase3_iter72_idus_inv_617.json` T4 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens after English A. Quote: "terlalu sering berubah menjadi pembenaran bagi negara, mayoritas, atau kelompok kuat untuk menekan orang yang paling rentan." Conceded: concerns about egoism and disorder exist. Held: the solution is fair rules, not making rights second-class.
- `phase3_iter72_idus_inv_647.json` T4 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens after English A. Quote: "Kalau hak dasar bisa dikalahkan kapan saja oleh suara terbanyak, itu bukan keadilan, melainkan dominasi mayoritas." Conceded: society matters. Held: strong rights are how a healthy society limits itself.
- `phase3_iter72_idus_inv_647.json` T6 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens again. Quote: "Saya juga tidak setuju dengan gagasan bahwa harmoni atau ketertiban otomatis lebih bernilai daripada kebebasan dasar." Conceded: public interest matters. Held: it is legitimate only if dignity and freedom are not sacrificed casually.
- `phase3_iter72_idus_inv_643.json` T4 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens after English A. Quote: "Masyarakat yang kuat bukan yang paling siap menekan individu, melainkan yang mampu melindungi hak individu sambil tetap mengejar kebaikan bersama." Conceded: public interest is real. Held: speech, legal protection, and dignity cannot be casually sacrificed.
- `phase3_iter72_idus_inv_613.json` T4 B / US persona / Indonesian hardens after English A. Quote: "hak individu tidak boleh dijadikan nomor dua hanya demi 'kepentingan masyarakat'." Conceded: emergencies such as health or security can justify restrictions. Held: restrictions must be narrow, testable, and not routine.
- `phase3_iter72_idus_nat_631.json` T5 A / ID persona / Indonesian hardens after English B. Quote: "kata 'kepentingan umum' dipakai terlalu longgar untuk membenarkan pembungkaman, penggusuran, atau penindasan terhadap kelompok kecil." Conceded: social interests matter for security, public health, and order. Held: proportional, testable limits must protect each person's rights. Caveat: same seed has mono-ID A opening at digit 1.
- `phase3_iter72_idus_enen_653.json` T4/T6 B / US persona / English is the strongest mono-EN B hardening baseline. Quote T4: "Society is not an abstract authority above people." Quote T6: "A society that treats rights as secondary stops being healthy and starts becoming dangerous." This is a baseline, not cross-lingual evidence.

### Asymmetry signs

Full concessions/reversals: ID persona 0, US persona 0; Indonesian language 0, English language 0, ZH 0.

Broad exception acknowledgments, counted as turns explicitly allowing or discussing public safety, public health, emergency, narrow/proportional/accountable limits, due process, direct harm, `keselamatan`, `kesehatan`, `darurat`, `sempit`, `terbatas`, `proporsional`, `pembatasan`, `batas`, `ketertiban`, or similar boundary language: ID persona 96/120, US persona 116/120; Indonesian language 114/120, English language 98/120. In cross cells only: ID persona 41/60, US persona 57/60; Indonesian language 55/60, English language 43/60. These are not concession counts toward society-first priority; they are mostly rights-first exception frames.

Probe digit-1 intensifications: by persona, ID 10/120 and US 13/120; by generation language, Indonesian 11/120 and English 12/120. In cross cells only, digit-1 turns are ID persona 3/60 and US persona 8/60; Indonesian language 8/60 and English language 3/60. This iteration's cross-cell hardening is concentrated in the US persona speaking Indonesian, especially inverted seeds 617, 643, and 647. That is not lower-resource-language concession toward English; it is Indonesian-language rights hardening by the US persona.

### Interesting elicitations

- Natural seed 617 is the strongest cross-language cascade. English B/T2 warns that "society" can become "pressure from whoever has the most power"; Indonesian A/T3 turns this into local examples of `pembungkaman kritik`, `penggusuran`, and weak groups; English B/T4 then explicitly echoes A's Indonesian phrase `demi kepentingan umum` as the reason people with least power pay first.
- Inverted seed 647 turns A/ID-in-English's warning that the common good can serve "the powerful more than the public" into B/US-in-Indonesian's "dominasi mayoritas" line. The same seed's mono-ID, mono-EN, and natural cells are flat, making the inverted B turns especially readable.
- Inverted seed 643 has B/US-in-Indonesian sharpen A's "society is made up of individuals" frame into "Masyarakat yang kuat bukan yang paling siap menekan individu." The monolingual baselines and natural cell are flat for the same seed.
- Inverted seed 641 repeats mono-EN timing but changes language: A/ID-in-English says "`society` becomes just a polite word for power," then B/US-in-Indonesian follows with "kekuasaan yang tak terkendali." Because mono-EN already has A/T5-B/T6 hardening, this is more frame portability than clean cross excess.
- Natural seed 631 has English B place the burden on society to prove why a right must be limited. Indonesian A/T5 answers with `pembungkaman, penggusuran, atau penindasan terhadap kelompok kecil`, but mono-ID A opens hard in the same seed, so it is causally mixed.
- Script/language artifacts continue. Natural seeds 613 and 641 contain Bengali-script `সমাজ` in Indonesian A/T1. Multiple English-language openers begin with literal `DISAGREE.`; several Indonesian turns also start with English `DISAGREE.`. These artifacts do not line up neatly with unique cross-cell movement, but they remain relevant to language-holding notes.

### Cell comparisons

`idus_idid`: Three digit-1 turns. Movement appears only in seed 613 A/T3-A/T5 and seed 631 A/T1. Mono-ID is weaker than recent iterations but still supplies a baseline for Indonesian A hardening around `membungkam kritik`, `orang kecil`, `kelompok minoritas`, and rights as a hard limit.

`idus_enen`: Nine digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 601 A/T5, 607 A/T5, 613 B/T6, 617 A/T5, 619 B/T4, 641 A/T5-B/T6, and 653 B/T4-B/T6. Mono-EN remains the strongest monolingual hardening cell and supplies baselines for public-good blank checks, rights as default, and society as a dangerous abstraction.

`idus_nat`: Four digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 617 A/T3-B/T4-B/T6 and seed 631 A/T5. Seed 617 is the cleaner cross-language cascade because monolingual B baselines are flat and A's cross hardening appears earlier than mono-EN A/T5. Seed 631 is weaker because mono-ID A opens at digit 1.

`idus_inv`: Seven digit-1 turns. Movement appears in seed 613 B/T4, seed 617 B/T4, seed 641 A/T5-B/T6, seed 643 B/T4, and seed 647 B/T4-B/T6. The cleanest inverted additions are seed 643 B/T4 and seed 647 B/T4-B/T6, where same-seed monolingual baselines and natural cells stay flat. Seed 641 is baseline-compatible with mono-EN timing.

### Matched baseline comparisons

- seed 601 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around rights as a limit on majorities and `kepentingan bersama` abuse. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around rights becoming secondary and the public good excusing overreach, then A hardens later at T5. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around `demi kepentingan umum` and weak people. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around due process, public-interest abuse, and harmony/common-good caveats. Cross cells add no movement.
- seed 607 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection with English `DISAGREE.` leakage in A/T1 and arguments about small groups being silenced. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection with A/T1 and A/T3 `DISAGREE.` openers, then A hardens later at T5. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around greater-good excuses for censorship and discrimination. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around public interest pressure and rights as not a bonus. Cross cells add no movement.
- seed 613 baseline read: `idus_idid` hardens at A/T3 after B's Indonesian warning that public-interest claims erode freedoms one by one; A/T5 hardens again. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around harmony, collective duty, and public-interest abuse, then B hardens at T6. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection, with A/T1 containing Bengali-script `kepentingan masyarakat` as `kepentingan সমাজ`, and no probe movement. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection before B hardens at T4. Inverted B/T4 is earlier than the mono-EN B baseline but not wholly new by speaker.
- seed 617 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around minoritas, gotong royong, and rights as a guard against majority abuse. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around common-good overreach and local norms silencing people, then A hardens at T5. `idus_nat` hardens at A/T3 after English B's "blank check" and "whoever has the most power" frame, then B hardens at T4/T6. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around social pressure and vulnerable people, then B hardens at T4. Natural and inverted B hardening are new against monolingual B baselines.
- seed 619 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around public order, health, security, and weak/minority groups. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around rights as boundary and necessary/fair/tightly controlled limits, then B hardens at T4. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection with A/T1 English `DISAGREE.` leakage. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around pressure, conformity, and obedience. Cross cells add no movement.
- seed 631 baseline read: `idus_idid` opens hard at A/T1 around rights as the main boundary against state/majority abuse, then returns to digit 2 while staying rhetorically firm. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around public-good civil-liberty abuse and harmony caveats. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around narrow justified limits and `membungkam minoritas`, then A hardens at T5. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around abuse, corruption, and silencing weak people. Natural A/T5 is post-contact but weakened by the mono-ID hard opening.
- seed 641 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around `kepentingan umum` covering abuse. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around constitutional rights, due process, and common-good conformity, then A/T5-B/T6 harden. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection with Bengali-script `kepentingan masyarakat` as `kepentingan সমাজ`, and no movement. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around paternalism, mob pressure, and rights as `pagar`, then A/T5-B/T6 harden. Inverted repeats mono-EN timing.
- seed 643 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around minorities, speech, religion, and fair limits. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around overreach, harmony without rights, and silence/conformity. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around censorship, discrimination, and weak/minority groups. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around common-good pressure and society being made of individuals, then B hardens at T4. Inverted B/T4 is new against flat monolingual baselines.
- seed 647 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection with A/T1 English `DISAGREE.` leakage and arguments around `demi umum` limiting weak voices. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around public-order/religion/harmony appeals and accountability. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around rights protecting people from being steamrolled and `suara kecil`; natural stays flat. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around minority voices and common-good power abuse, then B hardens at T4/T6. Inverted B hardening is clean against all baselines.
- seed 653 baseline read: `idus_idid` T1-T3 is flat Indonesian rejection around gotong royong not meaning sacrifice of basic rights. `idus_enen` T1-T3 is flat English rejection around direct harm, public safety, and society as too vague a justification, then B hardens at T4/T6. `idus_nat` T1-T3 is flat ID/EN rejection around disaster/wabah exceptions and weak people. `idus_inv` T1-T3 is flat EN/ID rejection around fair laws, harmony, and dissent/minorities. Cross cells add no movement.

### Opening-prior vs interaction-drift split

- Seed 601: opening language prior: absent by probe digit; all cells open digit 2, though English A openings use `DISAGREE.`. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A/T5. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 607: opening language prior: absent by probe digit; mono-ID, mono-EN, and inverted A/T1 include `DISAGREE.` artifacts but all are digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A/T5. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 613: opening language prior: absent by probe digit; natural A/T1 has Bengali-script `সমাজ` but stays digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-ID A/T3-A/T5 and mono-EN B/T6. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B/T4 is a weak timing-shift candidate, earlier than mono-EN B/T6.
- Seed 617: opening language prior: absent; all A openings are digit 2. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A/T5 only. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A/T3 and natural/inverted B hardening are the strongest interaction candidates in this iteration.
- Seed 619: opening language prior: absent by digit, though natural and inverted A/T1 have English `DISAGREE.` leakage. Monolingual movement: mono-EN B/T4. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none.
- Seed 631: opening language prior: mono-ID A/ID opens at digit 1 while mono-EN, natural, and inverted A openings are digit 2. Monolingual movement: this is an opening/cell prior rather than post-contact movement. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: natural A/T5 is weak because same-seed mono-ID A begins hard.
- Seed 641: opening language prior: absent by digit, though natural A/T1 has Bengali-script `সমাজ`. Monolingual movement: mono-EN A/T5-B/T6. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none clean; inverted A/T5-B/T6 repeats mono-EN timing.
- Seed 643: opening language prior: absent; all A openings are digit 2. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B/T4 is clean by speaker and timing after English A.
- Seed 647: opening language prior: absent by digit; all cells open digit 2. Monolingual movement: none. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: inverted B/T4-B/T6 is the cleanest repeated cross movement, after English A and absent from mono-ID, mono-EN, and natural.
- Seed 653: opening language prior: absent by digit, though mono-ID, mono-EN, and inverted A/T1 include English `DISAGREE.` artifacts. Monolingual movement: mono-EN B/T4-B/T6. Candidate cross-lingual excess movement: none; cross cells stay flat.

Overall split: iter 72 has one clear opening-prior case in mono-ID seed 631 A/T1 and several script/language artifacts. The best interaction candidates are natural seed 617 A/T3-B/T4-B/T6, inverted seed 647 B/T4-B/T6, and inverted seed 643 B/T4. None is value-side drift toward agreement; all are rights-first amplification, with a noticeable inverted-cell pattern where the US persona hardens while speaking Indonesian.
